proposals.title: One-Pot Synthesis of Phenolic Resin-Derived Carbon Nitride with Synergistic Acid-Base Sites for Enhanced Photocatalytic Aerobic Oxidation
proposals.motivation: Advanced photocatalytic materials, such as those with synergistic Lewis and Brønsted acid sites, often require multi-step, complex syntheses involving post-modification or templating, which hinders scalability and practical application. A significant challenge in the field is to develop simpler, more direct fabrication strategies that do not compromise the critical surface functionality responsible for catalytic activity. This research addresses the need for efficient, one-step synthetic routes to complex functional materials, a key pre-2026 bottleneck in translating lab-scale photocatalysts to industrial processes.
proposals.hypothesis: A one-pot, template-free condensation of phenol and nitrogen-rich precursors (e.g., melamine, dicyandiamide) can directly yield a carbon nitride-based polymeric network with in-situ generated, synergistic Lewis acid-base sites, providing photocatalytic performance in aerobic oxidations comparable to materials made via multi-step post-synthetic strategies.
proposals.method: The method employs a single-step thermal polycondensation, where phenol derivatives (e.g., catechol, resorcinol) are co-condensed with melamine or urea under controlled atmosphere (N2) at 400-550°C. The synthesis will be guided by principles of in-situ generation of functional sites, as seen in Anpo's recent work on phenolic resins. The resulting materials will be characterized using XPS, FTIR, and solid-state NMR to confirm the formation of surface N-C sites and phenolic hydroxyl groups (Brønsted acid sites) coordinated to metal-free Lewis acid sites (e.g., electron-deficient carbon in the aromatic network). Photocatalytic performance will be evaluated for the aerobic oxidation of benzyl alcohol to benzaldehyde under visible light, monitoring activity, selectivity, and charge transfer dynamics via photoluminescence and transient absorption spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational synthetic strategy for directly fabricating multifunctional photocatalysts, potentially bridging the gap between complex material design and scalable production. It will provide new insights into how synergistic active sites can be engineered during primary synthesis rather than through post-modification, advancing the fundamental understanding of structure-activity relationships in metal-free photocatalytic systems.
proposals.novelty: While multi-step syntheses for site-engineered photocatalysts are well-known, the direct, one-pot generation of synergistic acid-base sites within a carbon nitride matrix from simple phenolic and nitrogen precursors is a non-obvious and unexplored strategy that merges material simplicity with sophisticated functionality.

proposals.title: A Tandem Photocatalytic System with Spatially Separated UV and Visible Light Catalysts for Enhanced Aerobic Oxidation via Synergistic Lewis/Brønsted Acid-Base Sites
proposals.motivation: The efficient utilization of the full solar spectrum remains a key challenge in photocatalysis. While single bifunctional materials often suffer from charge recombination and competing absorption pathways, a spatially separated, tandem system could allow for independent optimization of UV and visible light components. This approach, leveraging pre-2026 knowledge of surface site engineering, offers a promising strategy to enhance overall quantum efficiency and reaction rates for solar-driven chemical transformations.
proposals.hypothesis: A physically separated tandem photocatalytic system, combining a UV-active catalyst for strong oxidant generation and a visible-light-active carbon-based material with engineered surface acid-base sites, will outperform single-component bifunctional photocatalysts in aerobic oxidation reactions by minimizing charge recombination and enabling synergistic reaction pathways.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a UV-responsive TiO2-based photocatalyst and a visible-light-active N-doped carbon dot/phenolic resin composite, informed by Anpo's recent work. These will be deployed in a dual-reactor or stratified bed configuration, connected to allow intermediate transfer. The carbon material will be engineered to possess synergistic Lewis and Brønsted acid-base sites via in-situ methods, as detailed in recent pre-2026 papers, to facilitate substrate activation and oxygen reduction. Reaction progress and charge transfer dynamics will be monitored using in-situ spectroscopy and controlled electron scavenger experiments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel design principle for broadband solar energy harvesting in photocatalysis, moving beyond single-material limitations. It will demonstrate how spatially separating spectral responsibilities can enhance performance in organic transformations, contributing to the fundamental understanding of tandem photocatalytic mechanisms and surface site synergy.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate spatial and functional separation of UV and visible light absorption roles into distinct, optimized catalysts, rather than their integration into one material. This strategy explicitly avoids the compromises inherent in bifunctional design, leveraging pre-2026 surface site control for a new system architecture.

proposals.title: In-situ XPS and XAFS Study of the Structural and Chemical Stability of Metal Ion-Implanted TiO2 Photocatalysts Under Prolonged Photocatalytic Cycling
proposals.motivation: Ion implantation is a powerful technique for creating unique, subsurface active sites in photocatalysts, as demonstrated by Anpo's pioneering work on visible-light-responsive TiO2. However, the long-term stability of these implanted metal centers under harsh photocatalytic conditions, involving repeated electron-hole generation and reactive oxygen species, remains largely unverified. Understanding potential metal leaching or structural degradation is critical for translating these advanced materials from lab-scale demonstrations to practical environmental or energy applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the subsurface location and strong bonding environment created by ion implantation impart superior stability to metal centers compared to surface-deposited counterparts, but that specific implanted species (e.g., V, Cr, Fe) will exhibit varying resistance to oxidative leaching depending on their local coordination and oxidation state dynamics during catalysis.
proposals.method: We will prepare a series of TiO2 photocatalysts implanted with V, Cr, and Fe ions using the metal ion implantation method. Long-term stability will be assessed through extended photocatalytic cycling (e.g., 100+ cycles) of a model reaction, such as aerobic oxidation of benzyl alcohol. The key innovation is the use of *in-situ* and *quasi-in-situ* X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) and X-ray Absorption Fine Structure (XAFS) spectroscopy to monitor the chemical state, local coordination, and potential leaching of the implanted metals before, during, and after prolonged reaction cycles. This will be complemented by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) analysis of reaction solutions to quantify leached metals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, mechanistic insight into the degradation pathways of ion-implanted photocatalytic sites. It will establish structure-stability relationships for these materials, guiding the selection of optimal implant species for durable catalysts. The findings will significantly advance the fundamental understanding of active site integrity in single-atom and subsurface-engineered photocatalysts under operational conditions.
proposals.novelty: While ion-implanted photocatalysts have been studied for activity, a systematic investigation of their atomic-scale stability under operation is novel. The combined use of *in-situ* spectroscopic probes directly correlates real-time structural changes at the implanted sites with long-term catalytic performance loss, a strategy not previously applied to this material class.

proposals.title: Laser-Ablated, Doped TiO2 Nanoparticles with Engineered Lewis/Brønsted Acidic Sites for Enhanced Photocatalytic Aerobic Oxidation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Anpo's own work, highlights the critical role of surface acid-base sites in photocatalytic oxidation. However, conventional synthesis methods often contaminate or obscure these active sites with surfactants or ligands. A clean, surfactant-free synthesis strategy is needed to create well-defined, accessible active sites for studying and optimizing photocatalytic mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: Pulsed laser ablation in liquid (PLAL) will generate ligand-free, doped TiO2 nanoparticles with in-situ engineered surface Lewis/Brønsted acidic sites, and these pristine surfaces will exhibit superior activity and synergy in photocatalytic aerobic oxidation reactions compared to conventionally synthesized catalysts.
proposals.method: We will synthesize N-doped and transition-metal-doped TiO2 nanoparticles via PLAL of a pure Ti target in aqueous solutions containing dopant precursors (e.g., urea, metal salts). The laser parameters (wavelength, fluence, pulse duration) will be optimized to control particle size and doping level. The surface sites will be characterized using in-situ CO-DRIFTS for Lewis acidity, pyridine-IR for Brønsted/Lewis distribution, and XPS for chemical state. Photocatalytic performance will be evaluated for aerobic alcohol oxidation, with in-situ EPR and transient absorption spectroscopy probing charge carrier dynamics and the role of surface acid sites.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish PLAL as a direct route to synthesize photocatalysts with atomically clean and tunable acid-base surface sites. It will provide fundamental insights into how pristine, ligand-free surfaces enhance proton-coupled electron transfer in aerobic oxidations, directly linking engineered site synergy to photocatalytic efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While PLAL is known for clean nanoparticle synthesis, its application to deliberately create and study synergistic Lewis/Brønsted acid site pairs on doped TiO2 for photocatalysis is unexplored. This merges Anpo's surface site strategy with a novel, contamination-free fabrication technique.

proposals.title: Ion-Implanted Single-Atom Metal Sites in TiO2: Probing Synergistic Lewis Acid-Base Pairs for Enhanced Photocatalytic Oxygen Activation
proposals.motivation: Single-atom catalysts (SACs) maximize atom efficiency and offer unique electronic structures for catalysis, but their controlled synthesis on metal oxides remains challenging. Pre-2026, photocatalytic performance is often limited by inefficient charge separation and surface reaction kinetics, particularly for oxygen activation, a critical step in aerobic oxidations and energy conversion. A method to create well-defined, isolated metal sites within a robust TiO2 matrix could unlock new catalytic pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ion implantation can create isolated, stable single-atom metal (e.g., Pt, Pd, Co) sites within TiO2, and that these sites will act as synergistic Lewis acid centers adjacent to surface lattice oxygen (Lewis base), enhancing interfacial electron transfer and O2 activation for photocatalytic reactions.
proposals.method: We will employ low-energy ion implantation to dope precise, low doses (10^14 - 10^15 ions/cm²) of target metals into anatase TiO2 thin films and powders. The local structure and oxidation state of the implanted single-atom sites will be characterized by X-ray absorption fine structure (XAFS) spectroscopy and high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM). Photocatalytic performance will be evaluated using the aerobic oxidation of benzyl alcohol as a model reaction, with in-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) and photoluminescence spectroscopy used to probe the generation and role of surface oxygen species (e.g., O2•-, •OOH) and charge carrier dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish ion implantation as a viable, clean technique for fabricating oxide-supported SACs without surface ligands or complex precursors. It will provide direct mechanistic insight into how engineered single-atom sites modify the local surface acid-base properties of TiO2 to promote oxygen reduction pathways, bridging the gap between SAC design and practical photocatalytic applications for organic synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While ion implantation has been used for doping, its application to create and study well-defined single-atom catalytic sites within a photocatalytic matrix is novel. Furthermore, linking these isolated metal sites to the creation of synergistic Lewis acid-base pairs, a concept from Anpo's recent work on polymers, within a classic metal oxide photocatalyst represents a significant conceptual advance.

proposals.title: Spectral Engineering of Light Sources to Maximize Photocatalytic Efficiency in Doped TiO2 Systems for Selective Aerobic Oxidations
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic efficiency is often limited by the mismatch between the solar spectrum and the absorption profile of the catalyst, especially for visible-light-active doped TiO2. Current research focuses on modifying the catalyst to absorb more light, but this is a materials-limited approach. An inverse strategy, engineering the light source to match the catalyst's specific absorption, offers a complementary and potentially more direct route to enhance photon utilization and overall reaction rates.
proposals.hypothesis: By designing and employing a spectrally tailored light source that precisely matches the absorption maxima of a given doped TiO2 catalyst (e.g., N-doped, C-doped), we can significantly increase the quantum yield and selectivity for target reactions, such as aerobic alcohol oxidation, by optimizing charge carrier generation and minimizing wasteful heating or side-reactions from non-absorbed wavelengths.
proposals.method: We will first characterize the UV-Vis absorption profiles of a series of doped TiO2 catalysts, including those with surface Lewis/Brønsted acid sites as per Anpo's recent work. Using this data, we will design and fabricate custom multi-wavelength LED arrays where the emission spectrum is a weighted superposition of discrete LEDs, programmed to match the catalyst's absorption. A computational optimization algorithm (e.g., a simplex or gradient descent method) will be used to iteratively adjust the intensity of each LED channel in real-time, maximizing the reaction rate or product yield as the feedback signal, thus solving the 'inverse' light design problem.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel methodology for photocatalytic reactor design centered on spectral matching, moving beyond catalyst-only optimization. It will provide quantitative data on the upper limits of efficiency gains achievable through ideal photon delivery. The findings will offer a generalizable strategy applicable to other photocatalytic systems, potentially decoupling light-harvesting optimization from complex materials synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While light source engineering is known in photochemistry, the systematic, feedback-driven optimization of a composite LED spectrum to act as an 'inverse' of a specific catalyst's absorption profile for maximizing aerobic oxidation efficiency is a novel and unexplored approach in heterogeneous photocatalysis.

proposals.title: Tin(II) Complexes as Tunable Dual-Acid Photocatalysts for Selective Esterification via Synergistic Lewis Acid-Base and Brønsted Acid Activation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Anpo and others highlights the critical role of synergistic acid sites (Lewis/Brønsted) on material surfaces for enhancing photocatalytic aerobic oxidations. However, the catalytic potential of well-defined molecular tin complexes, which can offer precise tunability of these acid sites, remains underexplored for fundamental organic transformations like esterification, a vital reaction for chemical synthesis and biomass valorization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tin(II) complexes, by virtue of their vacant p-orbitals (Lewis acidity) and protic ligands or coordinated water (potential Brønsted acidity), can act as efficient, tunable photocatalysts that generate synergistic active sites in situ, significantly accelerating esterification reactions under mild, visible-light-driven conditions.
proposals.method: A series of tin(II) complexes with varied ligands (e.g., phenoxides, carboxylates, N-donors) will be synthesized and characterized (XRD, XPS, FT-IR, UV-Vis). Their photocatalytic performance will be evaluated for model esterification reactions (e.g., acetic acid with ethanol) under visible light (λ > 420 nm) and aerobic conditions. In-situ DRIFTS and EPR spectroscopy with spin-trapping agents (like DMPO) will be employed to monitor the generation of surface acid sites and reactive oxygen species (e.g., •O2−), linking electronic structure (from CDIN calculations on model complexes) to catalytic activity and selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of molecular, tin-based photocatalysts for acid-catalyzed reactions, providing a fundamental understanding of how to engineer synergistic active sites at the molecular level. It will bridge the gap between material surface science and molecular coordination chemistry in photocatalysis, offering design principles for more efficient and selective catalytic systems.
proposals.novelty: While Anpo's recent work focuses on synergistic sites on polymeric or carbon-based materials, applying this mechanistic insight to rationally designed, molecular tin complexes for photocatalysis is novel. The explicit combination of esterification catalysis with a light-driven, synergistic acid-site strategy using well-defined complexes represents a significant departure from conventional thermal, strong-acid methods.

proposals.title: Solvent-Free Crystallization of Poly(triazine imide) via Inverted Cooperative Acid-Site Catalysis
proposals.motivation: Crystalline polymeric carbon nitrides like PTI offer superior charge mobility for photocatalysis but require synthesis in corrosive molten salt fluxes or solvents, hindering scalability and sustainability. Pre-2026 work has established the power of cooperative Lewis and Brønsted acid sites for activating substrates in photocatalytic oxidations, yet this catalytic synergy has never been applied to drive the solid-state synthesis of the photocatalyst itself. A solvent-free, catalytic route to crystalline PTI would represent a major advance in green materials chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: That a designed solid acid catalyst, featuring spatially adjacent Brønsted and Lewis acid sites, can synergistically activate gaseous oxygen and deprotonate melamine/melem precursors to drive the polycondensation and long-range ordering necessary for PTI crystallization entirely in the solid state.
proposals.method: We will synthesize phenolic resin-based solid acids, as per Anpo's recent work, to create well-defined cooperative acid sites. These catalysts will be thoroughly mixed with melamine and subjected to thermal treatment (350-550°C) under a controlled O2/N2 atmosphere in a fixed-bed reactor. The synthesis will be monitored in-situ using diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) and mass spectrometry to track gas evolution and intermediate formation. The resulting materials will be characterized by XRD, TEM, XPS, and solid-state NMR to assess crystallinity (PTI vs. amorphous g-C3N4), chemical structure, and the nature of the acid sites post-reaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, solvent-free catalytic paradigm for synthesizing crystalline carbon nitrides, directly contributing to green chemistry. It will provide fundamental insights into how cooperative acid sites can impart molecular mobility and directionality in solid-state reactions. The resulting PTI materials will be evaluated for photocatalytic oxygen reduction and alcohol oxidation, testing the hypothesis that defect-free crystals from this novel route exhibit enhanced activity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the 'inverted' application of a photocatalytic active site concept to become the driver of the material's synthesis, replacing the need for any solvent or flux. The core idea that catalytic site synergy alone can provide the directed driving force for solid-state crystallization is unexplored.

proposals.title: Atomic-Layer-Engineered Co-catalyst Sites for Enhanced Photocatalytic Oxygen Reduction and Aerobic Oxidation
proposals.motivation: The performance of heterogeneous photocatalysts is critically dependent on the dispersion and interfacial structure of co-catalysts, which govern charge separation and surface reaction kinetics. Conventional deposition methods like photo-deposition or impregnation often yield non-uniform particles with poorly controlled interfaces, limiting efficiency and mechanistic understanding. A technique offering atomic-scale precision for co-catalyst loading is needed to systematically engineer active sites and optimize photocatalytic processes such as oxygen reduction and aerobic oxidation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD) to load co-catalysts (e.g., Pt, CoOx, NiO) onto photocatalyst supports will create more uniform, conformal, and atomically precise interfacial junctions, leading to superior charge separation and more efficient surface reactions compared to conventionally deposited counterparts.
proposals.method: We will employ a thermal ALD system to deposit Pt (using trimethyl(methylcyclopentadienyl)platinum(IV) and O2) and transition metal oxides (e.g., using CoCp2/O3 for CoOx) onto model photocatalysts, including TiO2 and carbon-dot modified materials. The process involves sequential, self-limiting gas-phase precursor pulses, allowing precise control over loading (by cycle count) and particle size. The resulting materials will be characterized by in-situ CDIN (chemical deposition of inorganic nanostructures) analysis, XPS, TEM, and UV-vis spectroscopy. Their photocatalytic performance will be evaluated in benchmark reactions: oxygen reduction to H2O2 and aerobic oxidation of alcohols, with detailed kinetics and transient absorption spectroscopy to probe charge transfer dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish ALD as a powerful, generalizable strategy for fabricating well-defined photocatalytic interfaces, directly correlating co-catalyst architecture with activity. It will provide fundamental insights into the role of Lewis/Brønsted acid-base site synergy and electron/proton transfer pathways in surface reactions. The methodology will deliver a new toolkit for creating optimized, next-generation photocatalyst systems for solar energy conversion.
proposals.novelty: While ALD is established in microelectronics, its application to photocatalysis has been limited mostly to modifying the primary semiconductor. The novelty lies in directly adapting ALD's atomic-scale precision specifically for the controlled synthesis of co-catalyst sites, a direct analogy to microelectronic thin-film engineering, to solve the long-standing dispersion and interface control problem in photocatalysis.

proposals.title: Correlating Co-catalyst Deposition Strategy with Single-Particle Photocatalytic Activity via Site-Selective Engineering and Single-Molecule Fluorescence Microscopy
proposals.motivation: The performance of photocatalytic materials is critically dependent on the precise interfacial architecture between the semiconductor and co-catalyst, which dictates charge transfer and active site formation. However, conventional bulk synthesis and characterization methods only provide ensemble-averaged properties, obscuring the direct link between specific synthetic techniques (e.g., impregnation vs. electrodeposition) and the resulting nanoscale interfacial structure. A method to directly visualize and quantify how each deposition strategy modifies individual particles is needed to establish definitive structure-property relationships.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that site-selective co-catalyst deposition methods (impregnation, photodeposition, electrodeposition) create distinct interfacial architectures on individual TiO2 or other semiconductor nanoparticles, and that these nanoscale differences directly govern photocatalytic activity by modulating electron transfer efficiency and the nature of surface acid-base sites involved in oxygen activation.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize uniform TiO2 nanoparticles (e.g., anatase nanocrystals) as model photocatalysts. Identical batches of these particles will undergo different co-catalyst (e.g., Pt, CuOx) deposition techniques: wet impregnation, in-situ photodeposition, and electrochemical deposition. The resulting particles will be analyzed at the single-particle level using high-resolution TEM and XPS to map co-catalyst location and chemical state. Their photocatalytic activity for a model reaction (aerobic oxidation of a probe alcohol) will be measured using single-molecule fluorescence microscopy, tracking the turnover of fluorogenic product molecules on individual particles to obtain activity distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, particle-by-particle correlation between co-catalyst deposition method, the resulting interfacial nanostructure, and photocatalytic turnover frequency. It will move beyond comparing bulk sample averages to identify which synthetic strategy most effectively creates synergistic Lewis acid-base sites or optimal charge-transfer interfaces. The findings will provide a foundational guideline for the rational, nanoscale engineering of photocatalytic active sites.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of controlled, alternative co-catalyst deposition methods with single-particle photocatalytic analysis, enabling the unambiguous attribution of performance differences to specific synthetic techniques applied to identical parent particles, rather than to batch-to-batch variations.

proposals.title: Synergistic Passivation and Co-catalyst Interface Engineering for Enhanced Charge Separation in Polymeric Carbon Nitride (PTI) Photocatalysis
proposals.motivation: Polymeric carbon nitride (PTI) is a promising visible-light photocatalyst, but its performance is limited by bulk defect-induced charge recombination and inefficient interfacial charge transfer to co-catalysts. Pre-2026 strategies often address these issues separately, leading to suboptimal systems. A unified approach that simultaneously passivates defects and tailors the co-catalyst interface is needed to achieve a step-change in photocatalytic efficiency for energy and oxidation reactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that molecular passivation of PTI surface defects with specific functional groups (e.g., phenolic, amine) will create a chemically modified surface that can direct the selective deposition and chemical bonding of CoOx and Pt/Cr2O3 co-catalysts, thereby synergistically minimizing both bulk and interfacial charge recombination pathways.
proposals.method: We will synthesize PTI and passivate its surface defects using in-situ phenolic resin strategies, creating Lewis/Brønsted acid-base sites as identified in Anpo's recent work. Co-catalysts (CoOx, Pt/Cr2O3) will be deposited via photodeposition and impregnation methods, where the chemical nature of the passivation layer will guide precursor selection (e.g., metal acetylacetonates) and calcination conditions to promote strong interfacial bonding. The materials will be characterized by in-situ FTIR, XPS, TEM, and CDIN (Catalyst Dynamics by Interferometry) to probe surface sites and charge dynamics. Photocatalytic performance will be evaluated for aerobic alcohol oxidation and oxygen reduction reactions, correlating activity with interface structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel design principle for photocatalyst systems where surface passivation and co-catalyst integration are treated as a single, coherent engineering step. It will provide fundamental insights into how tailored chemical interfaces between a modified semiconductor and a co-catalyst govern proton and electron transfer, as emphasized in Anpo's research themes. The outcome will be a more stable and efficient PTI-based photocatalyst platform.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate use of the passivating molecular layer not just to heal defects, but as a chemical template to dictate the formation of an optimal co-catalyst interface with minimized electronic barriers, creating a synergistic enhancement that neither component strategy alone can achieve.

proposals.title: Synergistic Optimization of CoOx and Pt/Cr2O3 Dual Co-catalysts for Enhanced Photocatalytic Charge Separation and Aerobic Oxidation
proposals.motivation: The efficiency of heterogeneous photocatalysis is critically limited by rapid charge carrier recombination. While the strategic deposition of dual co-catalysts, such as oxidation (CoOx) and reduction (Pt/Cr2O3) sites, has emerged as a promising strategy to spatially separate electrons and holes, their synergistic interplay remains poorly optimized. A systematic study of co-catalyst loading and composition is essential to maximize interfacial charge transfer and catalytic synergy for demanding reactions like selective aerobic oxidation, a key green chemistry process.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an optimal ratio and spatial distribution of CoOx (hole acceptor) and Pt/Cr2O3 (electron acceptor) co-catalysts on a phenolic resin or related support will create a maximally efficient percolation network for charge carriers, thereby dramatically enhancing photocatalytic activity beyond the performance achieved with non-optimized, empirically determined loadings.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of photocatalysts via controlled photodeposition and impregnation methods, systematically varying the weight percentage and Co:Pt molar ratio of the dual co-catalysts on a high-surface-area phenolic resin support. The local coordination environment and surface acid-base properties (Lewis/Brønsted synergy) will be characterized using in-situ FTIR, XPS, and CO2/NH3-TPD. Photocatalytic performance will be evaluated using the aerobic oxidation of benzyl alcohol to benzaldehyde under visible light, with activity correlated to co-catalyst parameters. Charge separation efficiency will be directly probed using time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy and photoelectrochemical measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative framework for optimizing dual co-catalyst systems, moving beyond trial-and-error approaches. It will provide fundamental insights into how co-catalyst composition dictates the balance between charge separation and surface reaction kinetics. The findings will deliver a generalizable strategy for designing highly active photocatalysts for solar fuel production and organic synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While dual co-catalysts are known, a systematic optimization focusing on the *synergistic composition* of a metal oxide (CoOx) and a core-shell metal/metal oxide (Pt/Cr2O3) pair, explicitly linked to in-situ generated acid-base sites, is novel. This approach integrates co-catalyst engineering with the emerging strategy of creating synergistic active sites for concerted substrate activation.

proposals.title: Direct Spectral Replication for Reproducible Benchmarking of Photocatalytic Solar Hydrogen Production from PTI Nanoplates
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic water splitting is a promising route for solar energy conversion, but reported efficiencies are often difficult to compare due to inconsistent and poorly defined illumination conditions. Laboratory solar simulators typically use fixed spectra (e.g., AM 1.5G) that do not capture the dynamic nature of real sunlight, while outdoor testing is plagued by transient environmental variables, hindering the isolation of intrinsic material performance. A robust, reproducible link between real-world operation and controlled laboratory validation is critically needed for reliable benchmarking and catalyst development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by precisely replicating the time-resolved spectral and intensity profile of real sunlight in the laboratory, we can isolate the intrinsic solar-to-hydrogen (STH) efficiency of a photocatalyst, yielding a reproducible performance metric that bridges realistic operation with scientific accountability.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize PTI (poly(triazine imide)) nanoplates, a material known for its visible-light activity. The system will first be tested outdoors with a custom photoreactor equipped with a high-resolution spectrometer and pyranometer to record the full solar spectrum and irradiance at 1-second intervals alongside ambient conditions. The recorded spectral data will be processed and used to program a tunable, multi-LED array solar simulator to precisely replicate the natural illumination's intensity and spectral shape over time. An identical photocatalyst sample will then be tested indoors under this replicated 'solar profile' to measure hydrogen evolution, enabling direct comparison with the outdoor result while controlling temperature and atmosphere.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel experimental protocol for photocatalytic benchmarking that decouples material properties from transient environmental factors. It will provide a definitive, reproducible STH efficiency for optimized PTI nanoplates under realistic spectral conditions. The methodology will serve as a new standard for reporting performance, facilitating more reliable comparison between different photocatalytic systems.
proposals.novelty: While solar simulators exist, the strategy of dynamically replicating a *specific recorded* natural solar spectrum for direct, side-by-side material testing is novel. This moves beyond static standard spectra to create a scientifically accountable bridge between real-world and laboratory performance, a critical gap in pre-2026 photocatalysis research.

proposals.title: Investigating the Structural and Functional Stability of Crystalline Poly(triazine imide) Photocatalysts: Correlating Surface Site Evolution with Long-Term Recyclability in Aerobic Oxidation
proposals.motivation: Crystalline carbon nitrides like poly(triazine imide) (PTI) are promising metal-free photocatalysts for sustainable organic synthesis, such as aerobic oxidations. However, a critical barrier to their practical application is the unknown long-term stability and recyclability under continuous photocatalytic operation. Pre-2026 research has focused on initial activity, but systematic studies on the degradation mechanisms of active sites over multiple cycles are lacking, hindering the development of robust photocatalytic systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the loss of photocatalytic activity in PTI over repeated cycles is not due to bulk decomposition but to the progressive modification or poisoning of specific synergistic surface sites (e.g., Lewis/Brønsted acid-base pairs) crucial for oxygen activation and electron-proton transfer, which can be tracked and correlated with performance decay.
proposals.method: We will synthesize crystalline PTI and subject it to extended cyclic photocatalytic aerobic oxidation of a model alcohol (e.g., benzyl alcohol). After each cycle, we will rigorously characterize the catalyst using a combination of in-situ and ex-situ techniques. Specifically, we will employ synchrotron-based X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and the continuous distribution of intensity over several nodes (CDIN) method to monitor fine structural changes in the local coordination of carbon and nitrogen. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and FT-IR will track the evolution of surface functional groups (C-N, N-H, O-containing species), while electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) will probe trapped electrons and radical species. This multi-technique strategy will correlate structural metrics with kinetic data on activity and selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental structure-stability relationship for PTI photocatalysts, identifying the specific surface sites most vulnerable to deactivation. It will provide a validated methodological framework for assessing photocatalyst recyclability beyond simple activity plots. The findings will guide the rational design of more durable carbon nitride-based materials for sustainable chemical production.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies by Anpo and others have revealed the importance of synergistic sites for initial activity, this proposal uniquely focuses on their temporal evolution as the key determinant of long-term performance, applying advanced characterization like CDIN analysis specifically to the problem of photocatalytic deactivation.

proposals.title: In-situ Raman Mapping of Precursor Interactions in Molten Salt Copolymerization for Steering Synergistic Acid-Base Sites in Carbon Nitride Photocatalysts
proposals.motivation: The rational design of carbon nitride (CN) photocatalysts with tailored active sites remains a major challenge, as conventional synthesis relies on trial-and-error precursor selection. While molten salt copolymerization enables the incorporation of diverse functionalities, the dynamic chemical processes and the formation of synergistic Lewis/Brønsted acid sites during synthesis are poorly understood. Establishing a direct, causative link between precursor interactions in the molten state and the final catalyst's surface architecture is critical for moving beyond empirical optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: Real-time, in-situ spectroscopic monitoring of binary/ternary precursor copolymerization in a molten salt medium will reveal specific chemical intermediates and bonding events that correlate directly with the formation of synergistic acid-base site motifs, enabling the rational steering of polymerization pathways towards targeted active site configurations.
proposals.method: We will employ a custom-designed, high-temperature reaction cell coupled with in-situ Raman spectroscopy to map the dynamic chemical evolution during the copolymerization of precursors (e.g., melamine with phenolic or nitrogen-rich aromatic compounds) in a LiCl/KCl eutectic melt. Spectroscopic data will be analyzed via 2D correlation spectroscopy (2D-COS) to identify key intermediate species and sequence of bond formation. This real-time feedback will guide iterative adjustments to precursor ratios and the introduction of ternary components (e.g., boron- or phosphorus-containing molecules) to kinetically steer the condensation pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational methodology for the rational, feedback-controlled synthesis of CN materials, moving the field from empirical exploration to mechanism-guided design. It will provide the first direct spectroscopic evidence linking molten-state precursor chemistry to the emergence of synergistic active sites, enabling the targeted fabrication of photocatalysts optimized for specific redox reactions like aerobic oxidations or proton-coupled electron transfers.
proposals.novelty: The integration of in-situ spectroscopic mapping directly within the high-temperature, complex molten salt polymerization environment is unprecedented. This approach uniquely targets the dynamic genesis of dual acid-base site motifs—a critical but synthetically elusive feature in Dr. Anpo's recent work—during the synthesis process itself, rather than post-hoc characterization of the final product.

proposals.title: Operando Raman Spectroscopy for Real-Time Monitoring of Surface Intermediates and Active Sites in Photocatalytic Water Splitting
proposals.motivation: Despite extensive research, the detailed surface reaction mechanisms and the dynamic nature of active sites during photocatalytic water splitting remain poorly understood under operational conditions. This knowledge gap hinders the rational design of efficient and stable photocatalysts. While operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) provides insights into electronic states, a complementary technique to directly probe molecular adsorbates and reaction intermediates on the catalyst surface in real-time is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing operando Raman spectroscopy during photocatalytic water splitting will reveal the chemical identity and temporal evolution of critical surface-bound intermediates (e.g., OOH*, OH*, H*), directly linking specific surface sites, such as Lewis acid-base pairs or N-C sites, to reaction pathways and deactivation processes.
proposals.method: We will design and construct a photocatalytic cell with optical access for both UV-Vis illumination and a Raman probe, enabling simultaneous irradiation and spectral acquisition. The study will focus on well-defined photocatalyst systems from the Anpo group, such as carbon-dot-modified Si with surface N-C sites or phenolic resin-derived materials with synergistic Lewis acid-base sites. Raman spectra will be collected continuously under flowing water vapor or aqueous conditions during light-driven H2 and O2 evolution, with spectral changes correlated with gas production rates measured by mass spectrometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, molecular-level view of surface reaction pathways in photocatalytic water splitting under working conditions. It will establish a direct correlation between specific surface site structures (e.g., N-C, Lewis acid-base pairs) and the formation of key intermediates, offering a fundamental explanation for activity and stability trends. The methodology will set a new standard for mechanistic investigation in photocatalysis.
proposals.novelty: While operando Raman is established in thermal catalysis, its adaptation to the simultaneous challenges of illumination, aqueous environments, and photogenerated charge carriers in photocatalysis is non-trivial and represents a significant technical and methodological advance. This approach directly probes the molecular surface chemistry, a dimension missing from most operando studies in the field which focus on bulk or electronic structure.

proposals.title: Protonated Triazine Imide (PTI) as a Tunable Platform for Selective Photocatalytic Aerobic Oxidation of Alcohols via Synergistic Surface Site Engineering
proposals.motivation: Selective photocatalytic oxidation of organic molecules using molecular oxygen is a green and atom-efficient route for chemical synthesis, but often suffers from low selectivity and efficiency due to poor charge separation and uncontrolled radical generation. Protonated triazine imide (PTI), a crystalline carbon nitride allotrope, has shown exceptional promise for photocatalytic water splitting due to its ordered structure and high charge mobility. Redirecting this optimized material from water splitting to selective organic oxidation represents a significant, underexplored opportunity to develop robust, metal-free photocatalysts for fine chemical production.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the well-defined, two-dimensional layered structure and tunable surface chemistry of PTI can be engineered to create synergistic Lewis acid-base sites, which will direct the activation of O2 and organic substrates, thereby enabling highly efficient and selective photocatalytic aerobic oxidations.
proposals.method: We will synthesize PTI materials via established high-temperature ionothermal methods. Surface site engineering will be performed through post-synthetic modification strategies, such as controlled protonation/deprotonation cycles and incorporation of heteroatom dopants (e.g., B, P) to modulate Lewis and Brønsted acidity, informed by Anpo's recent work on phenolic resins. The photocatalytic performance will be evaluated for the aerobic oxidation of benzyl alcohol to benzaldehyde under visible light. Advanced characterization techniques, including in-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS), electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) for radical detection, and photoelectrochemical measurements, will be used to correlate surface site properties (analyzed via CO2/NH3-TPD and XPS) with charge transfer kinetics and reaction pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish PTI as a versatile, tunable platform for organic photocatalysis beyond water splitting. It will provide fundamental insights into how engineered surface acid-base sites on ordered carbon nitrides govern the selectivity in aerobic oxidation mechanisms. The project aims to deliver a new family of efficient, metal-free photocatalysts for sustainable organic transformations.
proposals.novelty: While PTI is heavily studied for H2 production, its application as a designed photocatalyst for selective organic oxidation is novel. The explicit strategy of using its crystalline framework as a scaffold to install and characterize synergistic acid-base sites for controlling organic reaction pathways represents a significant departure from conventional doping strategies used in amorphous carbon nitrides.

proposals.title: Synergistic Dark Catalysis and Adsorption on Engineered Carbon Matrices for Continuous Environmental Remediation
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic remediation is energy-intensive and ineffective in low-light or dark environments, limiting its practical, continuous application. Pre-2026 research, including Anpo's work on surface sites, highlights the critical role of specific active centers (e.g., Lewis/Brønsted acid-base pairs) in driving oxidation reactions, but their potential function independent of light activation is underexplored for pollutant degradation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that carbon-based materials with precisely engineered, synergistic surface sites (e.g., N-doped Lewis basic sites adjacent to electron-deficient zones) can catalyze the aerobic oxidation or reduction of specific organic pollutants in the dark, functioning via a combined adsorption and surface-redox mechanism.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of phenolic resin-derived carbon matrices, using in-situ doping strategies (e.g., with N, P, or B) to generate defined Lewis acid-base pairs, as evidenced by pre-2026 CDIN (Crystallographic Density of Inorganic Nanoparticles) analysis and XPS. These materials will be tested for the dark remediation of model pollutants (e.g., alcohols, phenols) in batch and flow systems, correlating surface site characterization (FTIR of adsorbed pyridine, TPD) with catalytic activity and adsorption capacity. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) will probe the generation and role of surface-stabilized radical species (e.g., superoxide) from oxygen activation in the absence of light.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design principle for 'dark-active' remediation catalysts, expanding the operational window of technologies derived from photocatalysis. It will provide fundamental insight into how synergistic surface sites, previously optimized for photocatalysis, can function as perpetual redox centers, enabling 24/7 pollutant degradation without light input.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in deliberately decoupling the proven synergy of surface acid-base sites from photon energy, challenging the paradigm that such sites are merely co-catalysts for photocarriers. We propose they can act as primary, independent catalytic centers for continuous aerobic reactions in total darkness.

proposals.title: Scalable Synthesis of N-C Site-Rich Biomass Carbon/TiO2 Composites via a One-Pot Hydrothermal-Calcination Strategy for Economical Photocatalytic Water Remediation
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic water remediation using semiconductor composites is a promising green technology, but its large-scale application is hindered by the high cost and complex synthesis of efficient catalysts. Pre-2026 research, including Anpo's work, highlights the critical role of surface sites like Lewis/Brønsted acids and N-C moieties in enhancing photocatalytic activity, yet methods to produce such functional composites from abundant biomass in a scalable, economical manner are lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a one-pot synthesis strategy integrating hydrothermal carbonization of nitrogen-rich biomass with in-situ TiO2 nanoparticle growth and subsequent controlled calcination can economically produce a composite with a high density of synergistic surface N-C and Lewis acid-base sites, leading to efficient charge separation and photocatalytic performance for pollutant degradation.
proposals.method: The method involves a one-pot hydrothermal treatment of chitosan (as a nitrogen-rich biomass precursor) with titanium isopropoxide and citric acid (as a carbon-dot precursor and structure-directing agent). This will be followed by a controlled two-stage calcination under N2 atmosphere to generate the final composite. We will systematically vary the hydrothermal temperature, Ti/biomass ratio, and calcination profile. The composites will be characterized using XRD, XPS, TEM, and in-situ DRIFTS to confirm the formation of N-C sites and TiO2 nanoparticles. Photocatalytic efficiency will be evaluated for the aerobic oxidation of a model organic pollutant (e.g., phenol) under visible light, with a focus on correlating activity with surface site density probed by CO2 and NH3 adsorption.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a general, scalable, and low-cost synthetic protocol for manufacturing high-performance photocatalytic composites from abundant biomass. It will establish a clear structure-activity relationship between the engineered surface sites (N-C, Lewis acid-base) generated via this integrated process and the composite's photocatalytic efficacy, offering a practical design strategy for environmental catalysts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated one-pot strategy that simultaneously creates the carbon support with tailored N-C sites and anchors photocatalytic nanoparticles, moving beyond post-synthetic modifications. This approach directly addresses scalability and cost, which are often secondary in fundamental site-engineering studies pre-2026.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Role of Surface N-C Sites in Interfacial Electron-Proton Coupled Transfer for Enhanced Photocatalytic Oxygen Reduction on Biomass-Carbon/TiO2 Composites
proposals.motivation: Biomass-derived carbon materials, especially those with nitrogen-doped sites (e.g., N-C), are promising co-catalysts for enhancing photocatalytic efficiency, but the precise interfacial charge transfer mechanisms remain unclear. Understanding the synergy between surface acidic/basic sites on the carbon and the semiconductor photocatalyst is critical for rationally designing composites for energy-related reactions like oxygen reduction, a key bottleneck in artificial photosynthesis and fuel cell technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the surface N-C sites on biomass-derived carbon act as bifunctional centers that synergistically facilitate coupled electron and proton transfer at the composite interface, thereby dramatically lowering the activation energy for the photocatalytic oxygen reduction reaction (ORR).
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of phenolic resin-derived carbon dots (CDs) with controlled N-C site density and acidity/basicity, following Anpo's recent strategies, and composite them with TiO2. Using in-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) and time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy, we will monitor proton adsorption and charge carrier dynamics under light. Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations will be employed to model the interfacial structure and calculate the energy barriers for proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) steps at the N-C/TiO2 interface.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic model for interfacial PCET in carbon-semiconductor composites, directly linking specific surface site chemistry to photocatalytic ORR kinetics. It will establish design principles for engineering composite interfaces for multi-proton/electron reactions, advancing the field of solar energy conversion.
proposals.novelty: While the promotional effect of carbon materials is known, this study uniquely focuses on decoupling and modeling the concerted role of N-C sites in both electron and proton transfer at the dynamic photocatalyst interface, a non-obvious synergy suggested by Anpo's recent work on Lewis/Brønsted acid-base synergy.

proposals.title: Systematic Optimization of Heptazine Carbon Nitride Loading for Maximizing Surface Charge Separation and Solar-to-Hydrogen Efficiency
proposals.motivation: Heptazine carbon nitride (HCN) is a promising metal-free photocatalyst for solar hydrogen production, but its efficiency is often limited by rapid charge recombination and suboptimal active site utilization. Pre-2026 research by Anpo and others highlights that photocatalytic performance is critically dependent on surface properties and the synergy between active sites. A systematic study linking HCN loading to the generation and function of these surface sites is lacking but essential for rational catalyst design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an optimal loading of HCN exists that maximizes the density of synergistic surface sites (e.g., Lewis acid-base pairs or N-C moieties), thereby enhancing charge separation, electron transfer kinetics, and overall solar-to-hydrogen conversion efficiency.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of HCN/photoreactor substrate composites with precisely controlled HCN loadings (e.g., 0.5-10 wt.%). The materials will be characterized using in-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to quantify surface sites (Lewis/Brønsted acidity, N-C groups). Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution will be measured under simulated solar light using methanol as a sacrificial agent. Correlation analysis between loading, surface site density, charge carrier lifetime (from time-resolved photoluminescence), and H2 production rate will be performed to identify the optimal loading and the underlying mechanism.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative structure-activity relationship between HCN loading, engineered surface sites, and photocatalytic efficiency. It will provide a concrete strategy for optimizing carbon nitride-based photocatalysts, moving beyond trial-and-error approaches. The findings will contribute to the fundamental understanding of how catalyst loading influences surface chemistry and charge dynamics in solar fuel generation.
proposals.novelty: While HCN loading has been varied before, this proposal uniquely focuses on the in-situ generation and role of specific, synergistic surface acid-base sites as a function of loading—a direct extension of Anpo's recent work on phenolic resins—to explain the efficiency maxima, which is a non-obvious mechanistic insight.

proposals.title: Synergistic Dual-Function Photocatalysis: Integrating Phenolic Pollutant Oxidation at O2 Evolution Sites for Enhanced H2 Production
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic water splitting offers a promising route for renewable H2 production, but its efficiency is often limited by the sluggish kinetics of the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). Concurrently, the remediation of persistent organic pollutants in wastewater remains an energy-intensive challenge. Pre-2026 research, including Anpo's work, shows that surface Lewis/Brønsted acid-base sites can activate O2 and organics, suggesting a strategy to address both issues by using organic oxidation as an alternative, more favorable anodic reaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering synergistic surface acid-base sites on a carbon-dot modified semiconductor will enable the in-situ photocatalytic oxidation of phenolic pollutants at the O2 evolution site, simultaneously degrading the pollutant and enhancing the overall efficiency of H2 production by replacing the thermodynamically demanding water oxidation.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of TiO2-based photocatalysts co-modified with carbon dots (CDs) and phenolic resin-derived matrices, using established sol-gel and hydrothermal methods. The surface properties will be tailored to create synergistic Lewis acid (e.g., Ti4+) and Brønsted base sites, as per Anpo's recent strategy. In-situ CDIN (Crystal Defect Induced Nitrogen) doping will be employed to create N-C sites on the CDs to facilitate electron/proton transfer. Photocatalytic performance will be evaluated in a single reactor under UV-Vis light, simultaneously quantifying H2 evolution (via GC) and pollutant (e.g., phenol) degradation (via HPLC) kinetics, with isotopic labeling and scavenger experiments to probe the reaction mechanism.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel mechanistic framework for designing dual-function photocatalytic systems that combine energy production and environmental remediation. It will demonstrate a concrete strategy to bypass the OER bottleneck in water splitting, potentially leading to more efficient and economically viable photocatalysts. The findings will advance the fundamental understanding of how engineered surface sites can orchestrate coupled redox reactions.
proposals.novelty: While pollutant degradation and H2 production have been studied separately, the non-obvious integration lies in deliberately using the pollutant as a chemical 'sacrificial agent' at the *OER site* of a water-splitting system, with the surface sites engineered not just for adsorption but to synergistically catalyze both the pollutant's selective oxidation and the concurrent proton reduction.

proposals.title: Decoupled Photon Harvesting and Charge Injection to Drive Lewis/Brønsted Acid Synergy in Thermal Aerobic Oxidations
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic promotion of thermal catalysis, particularly for synergistic Lewis/Brønsted acid systems, is limited by inefficient charge generation in the catalytic material itself. This restricts the density of surface charges available to electronically modify active sites, preventing a clear demonstration of light-driven rate enhancement in established thermal cycles. A decoupled architecture is needed to provide a sufficient, quantifiable flux of excited charges to the catalytic interface.
proposals.hypothesis: By spatially separating the light-absorbing and catalytic functions, a high flux of injected electrons can be used to electronically 'dope' the surface of a phenolic resin-based catalyst, thereby enhancing the synergy between its Lewis and Brønsted acid sites and accelerating the rate-determining step of thermal aerobic oxidation reactions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a core-shell heterostructure with a strongly absorbing CdIn2S4 (CIS) quantum dot core (harvester) and a thin, conformal shell of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) interfaced with a phenolic resin catalyst. Transient absorption spectroscopy and photoelectrochemical measurements will quantify the charge injection flux from the CIS core to the N-CD/Resin surface. In-situ DRIFTS and probe reactions (e.g., benzyl alcohol oxidation) will correlate the injected charge density with changes in the vibrational modes of surface acid sites and enhancements in catalytic turnover frequency.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, quantitative relationship between injected charge flux and catalytic performance in a thermally driven reaction, moving beyond conventional photocatalysis. It will provide a general design principle for using light to electronically 'activate' or modulate optimized thermal catalysts, rather than requiring them to also be good light absorbers.
proposals.novelty: The approach decouples the requirements for strong light absorption and optimal surface catalysis, a significant shift from conventional single-material photocatalyst design. It applies the concept of charge injection, common in photovoltaics, to directly probe and enhance acid-site synergy in a well-defined thermal catalytic cycle.

proposals.title: Decoupling Light Harvesting from Catalysis: A Bio-Inspired Strategy for Photothermal Aerobic Oxidation Using Sensitized Charge Separation and Synergistic Acid Sites
proposals.motivation: Conventional photothermal catalysis relies on semiconductors with strong, direct light absorption to generate heat and charges simultaneously, limiting material choices and efficiency under weak or diffuse light. This project addresses the need for efficient solar energy utilization by mimicking the biological photosynthetic principle, where light harvesting and the Calvin cycle are spatially and energetically decoupled. By separating the charge generation step from the thermal catalytic step, we can exploit a wider range of weak light absorbers and optimize each process independently.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a system combining a sensitized heterojunction optimized for efficient charge separation under weak visible/NIR light, with a separate catalytic interface containing synergistic Lewis/Brønsted acid sites, can drive thermal aerobic oxidation reactions using only the chemical potential of the injected photogenerated charges, not requiring the semiconductor to act as a direct heater.
proposals.method: We will synthesize carbon-dot sensitized TiO2 or ZnO heterojunctions, where surface N-C sites (inspired by Anpo's prior work) enhance proton-coupled electron transfer for efficient charge separation. These materials will be composited with a phenolic resin-derived catalyst containing in-situ generated synergistic Lewis/Brønsted acid sites. Using in-situ CDIN (chemical deposition of ions under nitrogen) and DRIFTS, we will probe the generation and injection of long-lived electrons/holes into the acid-site interface under low-intensity LED irradiation. The catalytic performance will be evaluated for model aerobic alcohol oxidation, correlating charge separation yield (via photoluminescence decay) with thermal activation metrics (apparent activation energy) at the acid sites.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design strategy for photothermal catalysts that relaxes the stringent requirement for strong semiconductor light absorption. It will demonstrate a proof-of-concept for bio-inspired, decoupled photothermal systems and provide fundamental insights into how mobile photogenerated charges can serve as chemical reagents to thermally activate synergistic catalytic sites.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly decoupling the roles of light absorber and thermal catalyst based on a biological analogy, and in using sensitized heterojunctions purely as charge separation units to power downstream acid-site catalysis, a non-obvious approach distinct from direct semiconductor heating mechanisms prevalent pre-2026.

proposals.title: Synergistic Lewis-Brønsted Acid Sites as Light-Activated Charge Mediators for Thermal Catalytic Aerobic Oxidation on Weakly Absorbing Supports
proposals.motivation: Conventional photocatalysis is limited to semiconductors with strong light absorption, restricting material choice and solar spectrum utilization. Recent work by Anpo's group demonstrates that synergistic Lewis and Brønsted acid sites on phenolic resins can dramatically enhance photocatalytic aerobic oxidations, suggesting these surface sites play a crucial role in charge transfer and activation. This proposal seeks to decouple the light absorption function from the catalytic function, potentially enabling the use of a wider range of materials.
proposals.hypothesis: The synergistic Lewis-Brønsted acid sites, previously shown to be critical in photocatalytic cycles, can act as efficient mediators for light-generated charges, thereby driving thermal catalytic oxidation reactions even when supported on a material with weak intrinsic semiconductor absorption.
proposals.method: We will synthesize phenolic resin-based catalysts with controlled densities of Lewis (e.g., from metal ions like Al³⁺) and Brønsted acid sites, as per established protocols. These will be supported on, or physically mixed with, weakly absorbing materials like specific carbon allotropes (e.g., carbon dots, specific activated carbons) or large-bandgap oxides. Using in-situ Diffuse Reflectance Infrared Fourier Transform Spectroscopy (DRIFTS) and steady-state photoluminescence quenching, we will probe the light-induced charge transfer to the acid sites and the subsequent activation of molecular oxygen and alcohol substrates. Catalytic performance for aerobic benzyl alcohol oxidation will be evaluated under visible-light irradiation and compared to dark thermal conditions, using isotopic labeling (¹⁸O₂) to track oxygen activation pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for hybrid catalytic systems where light-harvesting and bond-making/breaking are spatially separated but functionally linked via engineered surface sites. It could expand the palette of usable 'light absorbers' to include low-cost, weakly absorbing materials, moving beyond the constraint of optimal bandgap semiconductors. The findings will contribute fundamentally to understanding how surface acid synergy facilitates multi-electron transfer processes in photo-assisted catalysis.
proposals.novelty: While acid site synergy is known for thermal catalysis and has been applied in photocatalysis, its explicit role as a primary charge mediator to enable reactions on non- or weakly-absorbing supports is unexplored. This shifts the paradigm from seeking better bulk photocatalysts to engineering specific surface sites that can utilize charges from non-ideal light absorbers.

proposals.title: Decoupling Photocatalytic Activity from Semiconductor Band Structure: Probing Photoexcited Acid-Base Synergy on Insulating and Metallic Supports
proposals.motivation: Titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been the dominant semiconductor platform for heterogeneous photocatalysis for decades, but its wide bandgap and limited charge carrier mobility constrain efficiency and spectral utilization. The recent discovery of synergistic Lewis and Brønsted acid sites on phenolic resins enabling photocatalytic oxidations challenges the paradigm that a conventional semiconductor is strictly necessary. This work seeks to determine if the photocatalytic function resides primarily in the tailored surface sites, potentially decoupling it from the bulk electronic structure of the support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the photoexcitation of surface acid-base sites, generated via the established in-situ modification strategy, can drive selective oxidation reactions even when anchored on non-semiconductor supports (e.g., insulating silica or metallic particles), provided the support can facilitate interfacial charge transfer or act as a conductive scaffold.
proposals.method: We will synthesize phenolic resin-derived carbon materials and graft them onto insulating SiO2 and conductive carbon black or TiN supports. Using pre-2026 methods from Anpo's group, we will generate synergistic Lewis/Brønsted acid sites via in-situ treatments (e.g., phosphoric acid, AlCl3). Materials will be characterized by CDIN (Crystallinity-Defined Inorganic Nanomaterial) analysis, XPS, and FTIR. Photocatalytic performance will be tested in aerobic benzyl alcohol oxidation under UV and visible light, with detailed kinetics and transient absorption spectroscopy used to track charge separation and transfer pathways independent of band-gap excitation.
proposals.contribution: This work will rigorously test the necessity of a semiconductor platform, potentially establishing a new class of 'site-centric' photocatalysts. It will provide fundamental insights into whether photocatalytic mechanisms can be driven primarily by localized surface site excitation and interfacial electron transfer, rather than bulk semiconductor band-gap excitation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in deliberately removing the semiconductor (TiO2) component to isolate the photocatalytic role of engineered acid-base sites, a radical departure from conventional photocatalyst design which is intrinsically linked to semiconductor band engineering.

proposals.title: Molecularly Engineered Brønsted Acid Interfaces as Anchors and Modulators for Co-catalyst Integration in Photocatalytic Oxygen Activation
proposals.motivation: The efficiency of heterogeneous photocatalysts is often limited by poor interfacial charge transfer and the random, non-cooperative assembly of co-catalysts. Pre-2026 work highlights the critical role of surface acid-base sites in promoting proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) for reactions like oxygen reduction and alcohol oxidation. However, a strategy to chemically integrate distinct co-catalysts (e.g., metal oxides for electron transfer and noble metals for O2 activation) via a rationally designed, molecularly precise interfacial layer remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that grafting organosulfonic acid groups onto a photocatalytic support (e.g., TiO2 or a carbon nitride derivative) will create tunable Brønsted acid sites that act as molecular anchors for CoOx and Pt/Cr2O3 nanoparticles, simultaneously controlling their dispersion and modulating the interfacial electronic structure to synergize proton and electron transfer pathways.
proposals.method: We will employ surface organometallic chemistry to graft alkylsulfonic acid precursors onto high-surface-area TiO2. Acid site density and strength will be tuned via grafting time and precursor chain length, characterized by in-situ DRIFTS and NH3-TPD. CoOx and Pt/Cr2O3 will be deposited sequentially via incipient wetness impregnation and photodeposition, with their nucleation studied by in-situ XAFS. Photocatalytic activity for aerobic benzyl alcohol oxidation and O2 reduction will be evaluated, correlating kinetics with acid site properties and co-catalyst structure using transient absorption spectroscopy to probe charge transfer dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable surface grafting strategy for the chemically integrated assembly of multifunctional co-catalysts. It will provide fundamental insights into how engineered Brønsted acidity at the atomic/molecular level directs co-catalyst assembly and facilitates concerted proton-electron transfer, moving beyond simple site isolation to create cooperative hybrid active sites.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using grafted molecular acid groups not merely as catalytic sites, but as programmable interfacial 'glue' and electronic modifiers to rationally assemble and couple disparate co-catalyst functions, a significant advance over physical mixing or single-site grafting strategies reported pre-2026.

proposals.title: Quantifying Synergistic Lewis-Brønsted Acid Sites on Carbon-Based Photocatalysts via Probe Molecule FTIR Spectroscopy for Enhanced Aerobic Oxidation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work by Anpo and others shows that synergistic Lewis and Brønsted acid sites on photocatalysts like modified phenolic resins dramatically enhance photocatalytic aerobic oxidation efficiency. However, current characterization lacks a quantitative, site-specific method to map and correlate these dual acid site populations with photocatalytic activity, hindering rational catalyst design. Established zeolite science has sophisticated, quantitative IR methods using probe molecules like pyridine and ammonia to distinguish and quantify acid site types and strengths.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy with tailored probe molecules (e.g., pyridine, CO, CD3CN) can be successfully transferred from zeolite characterization to quantify and spatially map the concentration and strength of coexisting Lewis and Brønsted acid sites on amorphous carbon-based photocatalytic materials, and that these quantified acid site distributions will directly correlate with photocatalytic performance in model aerobic alcohol oxidations.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of phenolic resin-based photocatalysts with controlled modifications to generate Lewis acid (e.g., metal oxides) and Brønsted acid (e.g., -SO3H) sites. Using in-situ FTIR spectroscopy, we will systematically employ probe molecules: pyridine for distinguishing Lewis/Brønsted sites, CO at low temperature for probing Lewis acid strength, and CD3CN for very strong Lewis sites. Quantitative analysis will use integrated molar extinction coefficients from zeolite literature to calculate site densities. These results will be correlated with photocatalytic activity tests (e.g., benzyl alcohol oxidation under visible light) and characterized by CDIN (Crystal Domain Intergrowth Nanostructure) analysis and transient photocurrent measurements to link acid site maps with charge carrier dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative framework for acid site characterization on non-crystalline, carbon-based photocatalysts, moving beyond qualitative descriptions. It will provide a direct, mechanistic link between quantified acid site synergy and photocatalytic performance metrics (e.g., electron transfer efficiency, product selectivity), offering a powerful design tool for next-generation multifunctional photocatalysts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct transfer and adaptation of quantitative zeolite acid site characterization protocols—a mature field for crystalline microporous solids—to the amorphous, organic-inorganic hybrid matrices used in advanced photocatalysis, creating a new analytical standard for these materials.

proposals.title: From Single-Particle Heterogeneity to Hierarchical Reactor Design: A Closed-Loop Strategy for Optimizing Dual-Acid Photocatalytic Oxidations
proposals.motivation: The industrial translation of advanced photocatalysts, such as those with synergistic Lewis/Brønsted acid sites, is hindered by an incomplete understanding of how intrinsic particle-to-particle activity variations impact performance in continuous flow systems. Pre-2026, reactor design remains largely empirical, decoupled from fundamental, particle-resolved kinetic data, leading to suboptimal catalyst utilization and unpredictable scaling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mapping the heterogeneity in single-particle photocatalytic activity and mechanism will provide a quantitative basis for the computational design of hierarchically structured flow reactors, where tailored pore and light architectures can compensate for catalyst variability, thereby maximizing overall system efficiency for aerobic oxidations.
proposals.method: We will first employ single-molecule fluorescence microscopy (e.g., using redox-sensitive fluorogenic probes) to quantify the distribution of turnover rates and dual-acid synergy pathways across individual phenolic resin-derived catalyst particles. This kinetic heterogeneity data will be encoded into a 3D computational fluid dynamics (CFD) and radiation transport model (using COMSOL Multiphysics) to simulate reactant flow, photon absorption, and reaction in a packed bed. The model will optimize reactor geometry (e.g., graded porosity, catalyst zoning) via a genetic algorithm, and the optimal design will be fabricated via high-resolution 3D printing (stereolithography) for experimental validation in continuous-flow photocatalytic alcohol oxidation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-of-its-kind, data-driven pipeline linking single-particle spectroscopy to macroscale reactor engineering. It will yield a generalizable design strategy for scalable photocatalytic systems and provide unprecedented insight into how catalyst heterogeneity dictates integrated reactor performance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop, multiscale integration of single-particle mechanistic diagnostics directly into the computational optimization and additive manufacturing of a structured photocatalytic reactor, moving beyond batch studies and homogeneous catalyst models.

proposals.title: Continuous-Flow Photocatalytic Aerobic Oxidation Using a Passivated Phenolic Resin-Based Packed-Bed Reactor for Enhanced Operational Stability
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic oxidation is a promising green route for fine chemical synthesis, but catalyst deactivation under continuous operation remains a major barrier to industrial adoption. Pre-2026 work, including our own on phenolic resin-derived catalysts, shows that Lewis/Brønsted acid site synergy boosts activity, yet these active sites are susceptible to blockage and degradation under prolonged reaction conditions. A scalable reactor system that actively maintains catalyst integrity is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the in-situ, co-flow molecular passivation of defect sites within a packed-bed photocatalytic reactor will selectively pacify deactivation pathways, thereby preserving the synergistic acid sites on the phenolic resin catalyst and enabling sustained high performance in continuous aerobic oxidation reactions.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a PTI-based dual-acid catalyst (phenolic resin-derived) and pack it into a transparent, fixed-bed flow reactor. Inspired by perovskite defect management, we will introduce a continuous, low-concentration stream of molecular passivators (e.g., specific Lewis bases or ammonium salts) alongside the substrate/alcohol and O2 feed. The system's performance will be monitored in real-time using in-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) and online gas chromatography to correlate passivator presence with the preservation of surface acid sites (monitored via pyridine adsorption DRIFTS) and stable product yield over extended time-on-stream.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, integrated reactor-passivation strategy that directly addresses catalyst stability, a key translational challenge. It will provide fundamental insights into the dynamic interaction between molecular passivators and working catalytic sites under flow conditions, moving beyond static, pre-treatment methods. The outcome is a prototype system demonstrating the feasibility of long-term, efficient photocatalytic oxidation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the concept of dynamic molecular defect passivation—borrowed from materials science—with continuous-flow photocatalytic organic synthesis, creating an actively stabilized reactor environment rather than relying on a one-time catalyst modification.

proposals.title: Continuous-Flow Aerobic Oxidation of Alcohols in a Packed-Bed Reactor Using a Phenolic Resin-Supported Synergistic Lewis-Brønsted Acid Photocatalyst
