proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic aerobic oxidations offer a sustainable, energy-efficient alternative to traditional stoichiometric oxidants. However, batch-scale photocatalytic processes face significant challenges for industrial adoption, including inefficient light penetration, catalyst recovery, and mass transfer limitations. Translating promising dual-acid catalysts, which exploit synergistic active sites for enhanced performance, into a continuous flow system is a critical step toward practical application.
proposals.hypothesis: Immobilizing the recently developed phenolic resin-based catalyst, which generates synergistic Lewis and Brønsted acid sites in situ, within a transparent packed-bed flow reactor will enable efficient, stable, and scalable photocatalytic alcohol oxidation by improving photon and reactant mass transfer to the active sites.
proposals.method: The phenolic resin catalyst, proven for batch aerobic oxidations, will be synthesized and ground into a controlled particle size (e.g., 100-200 μm). A cylindrical glass reactor will be packed with the catalyst and integrated into a flow system with precise control of alcohol substrate and O2 flow rates. A key methodological focus will be optimizing reactor geometry, catalyst bed depth, and light source (e.g., LED array wavelength and intensity) to maximize photon absorption and minimize pressure drop. Reaction performance (conversion, selectivity) and long-term catalyst stability will be rigorously evaluated over extended operation times (e.g., >100 hours).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated prototype for scaling the novel dual-acid photocatalyst concept, demonstrating its viability beyond batch chemistry. It will yield critical engineering data on photon efficiency, throughput, and catalyst deactivation in flow, directly addressing the gap between advanced material design and process intensification for green oxidation reactions.
proposals.novelty: While flow photocatalysis is established, applying it to a catalyst system whose activity fundamentally relies on the in-situ generation of synergistic acid sites under reaction conditions presents a distinct and unexplored challenge, as the reactor design must preserve this delicate site generation and interaction during continuous operation.

proposals.title: Operando XAS Elucidation of Dynamic Synergy between Lewis/Brønsted Basic Sites and Metallic Co-catalysts in Photocatalytic CO2 Reduction
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic reductions, such as CO2 to fuels, are limited by inefficient multi-electron/proton transfer processes. While your recent work demonstrates that synergistic acid-base sites on carbon-based materials enhance photocatalytic oxidations, the role of engineered basic sites in modulating electron flow to metallic co-catalysts (e.g., Cu, Pd) for reductive chemistry remains poorly understood at the molecular level under working conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the protonation state and local electronic environment of adjacent Lewis/Brønsted basic sites (e.g., N-doped carbon, phenolate groups) dynamically regulate electron transfer from the photoexcited TiO2 or g-C3N4 support to metallic co-catalyst nanoparticles during photocatalytic reduction, and that this synergy can be directly observed and optimized via operando spectroscopy.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of photocatalysts where phenolic resin or carbon-dot matrices, functionalized with tunable N-containing basic sites (pyridinic N, -NH2), support well-defined Cu or Pd nanoparticles. These will be tested in a custom operando XAS cell for photocatalytic CO2 reduction with H2O vapor. We will employ time-resolved, in-situ X-ray Absorption Near Edge Structure (XANES) and Extended X-ray Absorption Fine Structure (EXAFS) at the Cu/Pd K-edge and N K-edge to simultaneously monitor the oxidation state/coordination of the metal and the electronic state of the basic N-sites as a function of reaction time and light illumination.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, operando spectroscopic evidence of how basic site protonation cycles couple with metal co-catalyst redox states to drive photocatalytic reduction. It will establish design principles for 'electron-proton transfer mediators' beyond simple co-catalysts, advancing the rational design of hybrid organic-inorganic photocatalytic systems for challenging multi-electron reductions.
proposals.novelty: While operando XAS is commonly used for metal centers, its simultaneous application to monitor the dynamic electronic structure of soft, light-element basic sites (N K-edge) adjacent to a metal co-catalyst under photocatalytic working conditions is a novel strategy to deconvolute their synergistic function.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Structural and Functional Stability of Surface N-C Sites in Si/Carbon-Dot Co-catalysts for Long-Term Photocatalytic Oxygen Reduction
proposals.motivation: The development of efficient, stable, and reusable photocatalysts is a critical bottleneck for the practical application of solar energy conversion technologies. While recent work, including Anpo's group, has demonstrated that Si/Carbon-dots (Si-CDs) with surface N-C sites significantly enhance photocatalytic oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) activity, their long-term durability under repeated photocatalytic cycles remains uncharacterized. Assessing stability is essential to move from proof-of-concept demonstrations toward viable catalytic systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the synergistic Lewis acid-base sites and the robust Si-C framework of the Si-CDs will confer exceptional structural stability, allowing the surface N-C sites to maintain high proton and electron transfer efficiency over multiple reaction cycles without significant deactivation.
proposals.method: The study will employ a combination of long-term cyclic photocatalytic testing and in-situ/ex-situ characterization. The Si-CDs co-catalyst will be subjected to extended periods (e.g., >100 hours) of photocatalytic ORR under standard conditions. After defined intervals, the catalyst will be recovered and its recyclability tested in fresh reaction mixtures. Key characterization techniques will include X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) to track chemical state changes of the N-C sites, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to assess structural integrity, and photoluminescence spectroscopy to monitor electron-hole recombination behavior after each cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset on the operational longevity of Si-CDs as photocatalytic co-catalysts, directly linking deactivation mechanisms (e.g., site poisoning, carbon oxidation, or structural collapse) to performance decay. It will establish critical metrics for the practical evaluation of carbon-dot-based photocatalysts and guide the future design of more robust materials for sustainable chemical synthesis and energy applications.
proposals.novelty: While most studies on novel photocatalysts focus on initial activity, this proposal systematically investigates the often-overlooked but crucial aspect of long-term stability and recyclability for the specifically engineered surface N-C sites in Si-CDs, a non-obvious extension of Anpo's recent findings on their initial synergistic activity.

proposals.title: A Microfluidic Photoreactor with Integrated Si/Carbon-Dot Co-Catalysts for Enhanced Photocatalytic H2O2 Production via Controlled Oxygen Reduction
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) production via oxygen reduction is a promising green alternative to the energy-intensive anthraquinone process. However, conventional batch reactors suffer from inefficient light utilization, mass transfer limitations of oxygen, and poor control over reaction intermediates. Microfluidic platforms offer superior mass/heat transfer and high surface-to-volume ratios, but integrating advanced, multifunctional photocatalysts like Si/Carbon-dots (Si-CDs) into such devices remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating Si-CDs, which possess synergistic surface N-C sites for proton and electron transfer, into a microfluidic photoreactor will dramatically enhance photocatalytic H2O2 production rates by ensuring intimate contact between reactants, catalyst, and light, and by stabilizing key oxygen intermediates through controlled flow dynamics.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a glass/PDMS microfluidic chip with a serpentine channel coated with a TiO2-based photocatalyst. Si-CDs, synthesized via established hydrothermal methods, will be immobilized within the channel alongside the photocatalyst to create integrated, high-surface-area reaction sites. The reactor will be irradiated with a controlled LED light source. We will use in-situ UV-Vis spectroscopy and electrochemical analysis to monitor H2O2 production and electron transfer efficiency under varying flow rates, oxygen partial pressures, and light intensities, correlating performance with the known Lewis/Brønsted acid-base synergy of the catalytic sites.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, efficient platform for continuous, on-demand H2O2 synthesis, directly linking nanoscale catalyst design (Si-CDs) to reactor engineering. It will provide fundamental insights into how confined micro-environments and flow control can optimize photocatalytic proton-coupled electron transfer pathways for oxygen reduction, advancing the field of continuous-flow photochemistry.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of Anpo's recently developed Si-CDs—with their specific surface sites for oxygen reduction—within a precisely controlled microfluidic environment, a strategy not yet reported for H2O2 production. This moves beyond batch studies to create a tailored, integrated system where catalyst properties and reactor engineering are co-optimized.

proposals.title: Photocatalytic Phenol Hydroxylation Using Water as the Sole Oxygen Source via Synergistic Lewis/Brønsted Acid Sites on Carbon-Dot Modified Photocatalysts
proposals.motivation: Conventional photocatalytic aerobic oxidations rely on molecular O2 as the oxygen source and terminal oxidant, which poses safety risks and energy costs for separation. Utilizing abundant and safe H2O as the sole oxygen atom donor for selective organic transformations represents a fundamental and practical challenge in green chemistry. This shift could enable safer, more atom-economical processes for synthesizing oxygenated chemicals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a photocatalyst featuring co-existing Lewis and Brønsted acid sites, in conjunction with surface nitrogen-doped carbon (N-C) sites that facilitate proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET), can activate H2O to generate reactive oxygen species capable of direct electrophilic hydroxylation of phenol's aromatic ring.
proposals.method: We will synthesize phenolic resin-based photocatalysts, modified with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs), using established in-situ methods to generate synergistic Lewis acid (e.g., from metal oxides) and Brønsted acid sites. The catalyst will be characterized via in-situ CO2-CDR (correlation of double resonance) NMR to map acid site distribution and dynamics under illumination. Photocatalytic tests will involve phenol oxidation in deaerated aqueous suspensions under visible light, using isotopic H2^18O to track oxygen incorporation into products (e.g., catechol, hydroquinone) via mass spectrometry. Charge separation efficiency will be probed by transient absorption spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel photocatalytic pathway for aromatic C–H hydroxylation using H2O, moving beyond O2-dependent mechanisms. It will provide fundamental insights into how synergistic acid sites and CD-facilitated PCET channels cooperate to activate H2O. The findings could lead to safer, more sustainable photocatalytic oxidation strategies.
proposals.novelty: While Anpo's prior work used synergistic acid sites with O2, this proposal uniquely applies this concept to utilize H2O as the sole oxygen source, a significant mechanistic departure. The integration of CD-modified PCET pathways specifically for H2O activation in organic synthesis is unexplored in this context.

proposals.title: Photocatalytic Phenol Reduction to Benzene via Synergistic Surface Sites on Oxygen-Vacancy-Rich Catalysts
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic oxidation of benzene to phenol is a well-studied green chemistry route, but the reverse, selective reduction of phenol to benzene, remains a significant challenge. This reaction is crucial for sustainable chemical recycling, enabling the deoxygenation and recovery of aromatic feedstocks from biomass-derived phenolics. Achieving this under mild, photocatalytic conditions would represent a major advance in energy-efficient chemical transformations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that oxygen-vacancy (OV)-rich catalysts, previously used for aerobic oxidations, can be repurposed to drive the photocatalytic reduction of phenol to benzene by providing synergistic Lewis acid-base sites that facilitate C-O bond cleavage and hydrogenation.
proposals.method: We will synthesize OV-rich TiO2 and other metal oxides via established methods (e.g., hydrogen reduction, doping). Phenolic resin-derived carbon materials, as used in Anpo's recent work, will be integrated to create surface N-C sites and modulate electron transfer. The catalysts will be characterized using in-situ FTIR, XPS, and photoluminescence to probe surface sites and charge dynamics. Photocatalytic tests will be conducted in a gas-solid or liquid-phase reactor under UV/visible light, using water or a mild hydrogen donor (e.g., formic acid) as the reductant, with product analysis by GC-MS.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new photocatalytic pathway for aromatic deoxygenation, expanding the functional scope of OV-based photocatalysts beyond oxidation. It will provide fundamental insights into how synergistic acid-base sites on OV-rich surfaces can be tuned to drive reductive C-O bond scission, contributing to the design principles for 'inverse' photocatalytic processes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying catalysts designed for aerobic oxidation to their inverse, reductive function, specifically targeting phenol deoxygenation—a reaction rarely achieved photocatalytically. The strategy leverages pre-2026 knowledge of synergistic sites but reverses the electron flow and reaction outcome.

proposals.title: Oxygen Vacancy-Rich TiO2 Catalysts for the Photocatalytic C-H Activation and Partial Oxidation of Light Alkanes
proposals.motivation: The selective, low-temperature functionalization of inert aliphatic C-H bonds in alkanes, such as methane and ethane, remains a major challenge in catalysis. Photocatalysis offers a sustainable route using light energy, but existing systems often lack the necessary activation strength for these strong bonds. The development of efficient, selective catalysts for alkane oxidation is crucial for upgrading natural gas and light hydrocarbons into valuable oxygenated chemicals under mild conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineered oxygen vacancies (OVs) on TiO2 surfaces will act as synergistic Lewis acid-base sites, effectively weakening C-H bonds in alkanes and facilitating the transfer of photogenerated holes and electrons for selective oxidation using molecular oxygen.
proposals.method: We will synthesize OV-rich TiO2 catalysts via controlled hydrogen reduction or doping strategies, as established in the Anpo group. The catalysts will be characterized using in-situ CDIN (Chemical Deposition of Inorganic Nanostructures) to probe surface sites, alongside XRD, XPS, and EPR to confirm OV concentration and electronic structure. Photocatalytic performance will be evaluated for the oxidation of propane and ethane under UV and visible light, using O2 as the oxidant. Product distribution (alcohols, aldehydes, CO2) and reaction kinetics will be analyzed by GC/MS to correlate OV density with activity/selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct structure-activity relationship between OV concentration and photocatalytic alkane activation efficiency. It will provide a new mechanistic understanding of how dual acid-base sites on OV-rich surfaces synergize with photogenerated carriers for challenging C-H bond scission, extending the application of vacancy engineering beyond aromatic systems.
proposals.novelty: While OV-rich catalysts are known for activating aromatic C-H bonds and O2, their systematic application to the more challenging photocatalytic activation of aliphatic alkanes is novel. The explicit strategy of using OVs to generate the synergistic Lewis acid-base functionality observed in phenolic resin systems for this new substrate class is a non-obvious and innovative extension.

proposals.title: Synergistic Active Sites on Carbon Nitride: Trace Metal Atoms and Surface N-C Sites for Enhanced Photocatalytic Oxygen Reduction
proposals.motivation: Metal-free carbon-based photocatalysts, such as carbon nitride and carbon-dots, are promising for sustainable energy applications like the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). However, their performance is often limited by inefficient charge separation and sluggish reaction kinetics. Introducing controlled, trace amounts of metal atoms could synergistically enhance the activity of established carbon/nitrogen active sites, moving beyond the strict metal-free paradigm to unlock superior catalytic systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strategic introduction of trace, atomically dispersed transition metals (e.g., Fe, Co) into a carbon nitride framework co-doped with nitrogen-rich carbon-dots will create synergistic Lewis acid (metal site) and Brønsted/Lewis base (N-C site) pairs. This synergy will significantly enhance interfacial proton-coupled electron transfer, thereby boosting photocatalytic ORR activity and selectivity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of photocatalysts via a co-condensation and in-situ generation strategy. Phenolic resin and nitrogen-rich precursors will be polymerized with precise trace amounts of transition metal salts. Using in-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) and CO-probe FTIR, we will characterize the generation of synergistic Lewis acid-base sites. The electronic structure and charge transfer dynamics will be probed using steady-state and time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy, complemented by in-situ photoelectrochemical measurements to correlate site generation with ORR performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for hybrid active sites in carbon-based photocatalysts, demonstrating how trace metals can be used not as primary catalytic centers but as synergistic modifiers. It will provide a fundamental understanding of proton-electron transfer mechanisms at metal-N-C interfaces, contributing to the rational design of advanced catalysts for solar energy conversion.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in deliberately relaxing the 'metal-free' constraint to use trace metals as synergistic co-catalysts with surface N-C sites, rather than as conventional nanoparticle co-catalysts or replacement active centers, creating a new class of hybrid active sites for photocatalysis.

proposals.title: First-Principles Design of Carbon-Doped Photocatalysts: Mapping Doping Concentration and Spatial Configuration to Band Gap and Surface Reactivity
proposals.motivation: Carbon doping is a key strategy to engineer the band structure and surface sites of photocatalysts, as evidenced by Anpo's work on N-C sites for oxygen reduction. However, pre-2026 experimental studies often treat doping empirically, lacking a systematic atomic-level understanding of how specific doping concentrations and spatial patterns (e.g., isolated vs. clustered C atoms) govern electronic properties and the generation of synergistic active sites for photocatalytic oxidations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an optimal carbon doping concentration and spatial configuration exists that minimizes band gap for visible-light absorption while simultaneously creating a favorable surface electronic environment, enhancing charge separation and the formation of synergistic Lewis acid-base sites for aerobic oxidation reactions.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) calculations with hybrid functionals (e.g., HSE06) to model carbon-doped oxide models (e.g., TiO2, SiO2-based phenolic resin analogs). A high-throughput computational screening strategy will be implemented, systematically varying C doping levels (e.g., 0.5-5 at.%) and spatial configurations (substitutional, interstitial, clustered patterns). The Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) will be used to calculate band structures, density of states, Bader charges, and adsorption energies of key intermediates (e.g., O2, alcohols) to correlate doping patterns with photocatalytic descriptors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, atomistic design map linking carbon doping parameters to photocatalytic performance metrics. It will offer predictive guidelines for synthesizing next-generation doped photocatalysts with tailored band gaps and optimized surface sites for energy-related reactions, specifically advancing the rational design of materials for solar-driven organic transformations.
proposals.novelty: While carbon doping is known, this study's novelty lies in its systematic, high-throughput computational dissection of both concentration *and* spatial configuration—a non-obvious factor—as independent variables to decouple their effects on bulk electronic properties and surface catalytic sites, directly informing Anpo's experimental strategy for creating synergistic active centers.

proposals.title: Probing the Stability of Carbon-Doped p-BN Photocatalysts: In-situ Characterization of Surface Sites and Degradation Pathways Under Simulated Water Splitting Conditions
proposals.motivation: Carbon-doped porous boron nitride (p-BN) has emerged as a promising metal-free photocatalyst for water splitting due to its tunable band gap and high surface area. However, the long-term stability of its active sites—particularly under the harsh, oxidizing conditions of photocatalytic water oxidation—remains a critical, unaddressed challenge. Assessing this stability is essential for translating laboratory-scale activity into practical, durable photocatalytic systems for solar energy conversion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that carbon-doped p-BN will exhibit gradual degradation of its surface Lewis acid-base sites and carbon-nitrogen (C-N) moieties under prolonged illumination in aqueous media, and that this degradation is the primary cause of photocatalytic deactivation for water splitting.
proposals.method: We will synthesize carbon-doped p-BN via established thermal condensation methods. Long-term stability will be assessed in a custom photoreactor with simulated solar illumination (AM 1.5G) and continuous monitoring of H2/O2 evolution. In-situ and post-reaction characterization will be central: we will employ in-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) to monitor surface functional groups, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to track chemical state changes of B, N, and C, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to observe structural evolution. The synergy of Lewis acid (B sites) and Brønsted base (N sites) will be probed using probe molecule adsorption, correlating site loss with activity decay.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed mechanistic understanding of the degradation pathways for carbon-doped p-BN photocatalysts in operando. It will establish clear structure-stability-activity relationships, guiding the rational design of more robust, non-oxide photocatalysts. The findings will contribute critical knowledge on the durability of metal-free systems for solar fuel production.
proposals.novelty: While most studies focus on initial activity, this proposal uniquely targets the stability of specific, synergistic active sites (Lewis acid-base pairs and C-N motifs) under reaction conditions, directly extending Anpo's recent work on synergistic sites in phenolic resins to a high-stability BN matrix for a more demanding photocatalytic reaction.

proposals.title: Operando Raman Spectroscopy Elucidating the Dynamic Role of Doped Boron Nitride Surface Sites in Photocatalytic Water Splitting
proposals.motivation: Understanding the dynamic chemical state of active sites during photocatalysis, rather than just before or after reaction, is a critical frontier for rationally designing efficient materials. While operando spectroscopic techniques like Raman are well-established in electrocatalysis, their application to complex photocatalytic systems, especially for non-oxide materials like doped boron nitride (BN), remains underexplored. This gap limits our ability to correlate real-time surface transformations with photocatalytic performance metrics like hydrogen evolution rates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that operando Raman spectroscopy will directly reveal that the photocatalytic water splitting activity of doped BN is governed by the in-situ generation and interconversion of synergistic Lewis acid-base sites (e.g., B and N centers with modified electron density) under reaction conditions, which facilitate proton-coupled electron transfer processes.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of metal- and non-metal-doped hexagonal BN (h-BN) catalysts using established methods. A custom-designed photocatalytic reactor cell compatible with a Renishaw inVia Raman microscope will be constructed, allowing simultaneous UV-Vis irradiation (λ ≥ 300 nm), water vapor or liquid water introduction, and gas product analysis via mass spectrometry. Operando Raman spectra will be acquired continuously during photocatalytic water splitting, tracking changes in B-N vibrational modes, dopant-related bands, and potential surface intermediate species (e.g., OOH, OH). These spectroscopic dynamics will be correlated with the simultaneous measurement of H2 and O2 evolution rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, real-time spectroscopic evidence of the chemical state evolution of doped BN during photocatalysis, moving beyond post-mortem analysis. It will establish a direct structure-activity relationship by linking specific in-situ spectral features to catalytic turnover, thereby identifying the true active sites. The developed operando methodology will be a transferable strategy for studying other non-oxide photocatalysts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting a powerful operando methodology from electrocatalysis to probe the dynamic surface chemistry of a photocatalytic system where charge carrier generation and surface reactions are intricately coupled. Focusing on doped BN, a material with emerging potential for water splitting, to monitor its Lewis acid-base site synergy in real-time is a significant and non-obvious advance over static characterization.

proposals.title: Beyond Oxygen Vacancies: Exploring Surface N-C Sites and Lewis Acid-Base Pairs for Selective Two-Electron Oxygen Reduction on Carbon-Based Photocatalysts
proposals.motivation: The photocatalytic two-electron oxygen reduction reaction (2e- ORR) to produce H2O2 is a promising green synthesis route. Pre-2026 research heavily emphasizes the critical role of oxygen vacancies (OVs) in activating O2 and steering the selectivity toward the 2e- pathway. However, the necessity of OVs remains an open question, and their instability under operational conditions is a known limitation. This project challenges this paradigm by investigating alternative, potentially more robust, surface sites.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, non-OV surface sites, such as nitrogen-coordinated carbon (N-C) motifs and in-situ generated synergistic Lewis acid-base pairs, can effectively promote the adsorption and activation of O2 for the selective 2e- reduction pathway, circumventing the need for conventional oxygen vacancies.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of carbon-dot modified Si-based materials and phenolic resin-derived photocatalysts, building on Anpo's recent work. Using controlled synthesis and doping strategies, we will create surfaces rich in defined N-C sites (e.g., pyridinic N) and Lewis acid-base pairs while intentionally minimizing OV formation. These materials will be characterized by in-situ FTIR, XPS, and CO2-TPD to confirm site identity and probe surface acidity/basicity. Photocatalytic 2e- ORR performance (H2O2 yield, selectivity) will be correlated with surface site density via kinetic analysis and in-situ spectroscopic monitoring (CDIN) to establish structure-activity relationships and elucidate the electron/proton transfer mechanisms at these alternative sites.
proposals.contribution: This work will expand the fundamental understanding of active sites for photocatalytic 2e- ORR by demonstrating that OVs are not a prerequisite. It will provide a new design strategy based on engineering stable N-C configurations and acid-base pairs, leading to more durable photocatalysts for H2O2 production. The findings will directly challenge and refine the prevailing surface defect paradigm in photocatalytic oxygen activation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically decoupling the 2e- ORR activity from oxygen vacancies and attributing it to other well-defined surface functionalities, a direct challenge to a common assumption in the field. It uniquely combines the concepts of N-C sites for electron transfer and Lewis acid-base pairs for substrate activation, both emerging in Anpo's recent work, for a new photocatalytic application.

proposals.title: Single-Atom Metal Sites on TiO2 for Steering Photocatalytic Oxygen Reduction Pathways via Controlled Electron Transfer
proposals.motivation: The photocatalytic reduction of O2 on TiO2 is a critical reaction for renewable energy and chemical synthesis, but it suffers from poor selectivity, often yielding a mixture of reactive oxygen species (ROS) like superoxide (O2•−) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Pre-2026, single-atom catalysis (SAC) has emerged in electrocatalysis as a powerful strategy to precisely control reaction pathways by tailoring the local coordination environment of isolated metal atoms. Applying this SAC concept to photocatalytic TiO2 systems offers a promising route to solve the selectivity challenge in O2 activation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that anchoring single transition metal atoms (e.g., Pt, Co, Fe) onto TiO2 surfaces will create well-defined, isolated active sites that can selectively steer the multi-electron O2 reduction pathway by modulating the electron transfer kinetics and intermediate adsorption, moving beyond the non-selective ROS generation of pristine TiO2.
proposals.method: We will synthesize single-atom catalysts (M1/TiO2, M = Pt, Co, Fe) using a photodeposition strategy combined with a spatial confinement method, leveraging pre-2026 expertise in creating surface N-C sites on carbon-dots. The atomic dispersion and coordination environment will be characterized by aberration-corrected HAADF-STEM and X-ray absorption fine structure (XAFS) spectroscopy. Photocatalytic O2 reduction performance and pathway selectivity (towards H2O2 vs. H2O) will be evaluated under UV-vis irradiation, using in-situ electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and scavenger tests to identify key intermediates. The synergy between the single-atom metal sites (acting as Lewis acids for O2 activation) and surface hydroxyls/Ti sites (Brønsted/Lewis acid-base pairs) will be probed via in-situ FTIR of adsorbed probe molecules.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct structure-activity relationship between the coordination geometry of single metal atoms on TiO2 and the selectivity of photocatalytic O2 reduction, providing a design principle for SAC in photocatalysis. It will demonstrate how concepts from electrocatalytic O2 reduction can be transposed and uniquely adapted to photocatalytic systems governed by photo-generated electron-hole pairs.
proposals.novelty: While SAC for electrocatalytic O2 reduction is known, its application to *photocatalytic* O2 reduction on TiO2 to control pathway selectivity is novel. The proposal uniquely integrates Anpo's expertise in surface acid-base sites and in-situ characterization with the emerging SAC strategy, moving from non-selective surface sites to precisely engineered single-atom centers.

proposals.title: Engineering a Continuous-Flow Photoreactor with Oxygen-Vacancy-Rich TiO2 for Scalable Photocatalytic Aerobic Oxidations
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic aerobic oxidations using TiO2-based catalysts offer a green route for fine chemical synthesis, but their industrial application is hindered by batch reactor limitations, including poor photon and mass transfer and catalyst handling. Continuous flow systems can overcome these issues, yet their design for heterogeneous photocatalysts, especially those relying on surface oxygen vacancy (OV) sites for O2 activation and charge separation, remains underdeveloped. Translating the proven efficacy of OV-rich TiO2 catalysts from batch to continuous flow is a critical step toward practical, energy-efficient chemical production.
proposals.hypothesis: A rationally designed continuous-flow photoreactor, integrating an immobilized OV-rich TiO2 catalyst, will provide superior and stable performance in model aerobic oxidation reactions (e.g., alcohol to aldehyde) compared to batch systems, due to enhanced illumination efficiency, improved reactant-catalyst contact, and effective utilization of surface OV sites for oxygen reduction.
proposals.method: The project will first synthesize and characterize OV-rich TiO2 catalysts (e.g., via thermal reduction or metal doping) and confirm the presence of OV sites using techniques like Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). A tubular continuous-flow photoreactor will be engineered with the catalyst immobilized on the reactor walls or on a structured monolith. The reactor design will prioritize uniform high-intensity LED illumination and precise control of residence time, oxygen pressure, and flow dynamics. Performance will be evaluated for a model reaction like benzyl alcohol oxidation, with conversion, selectivity, and long-term stability compared directly to an equivalent batch system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated reactor engineering blueprint for scaling photocatalytic processes that depend on surface defect sites. It will deliver quantitative data on the synergy between OV-rich catalyst properties and continuous-flow operation, establishing key design principles for photon, mass, and electron transfer in scalable systems. The results will bridge the gap between advanced catalyst development and practical reactor engineering for sustainable chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While continuous flow photocatalysis is known, the specific design for maximizing the function of surface oxygen vacancies—critical for O2 activation and electron-hole separation—in an immobilized, engineered reactor is unexplored. This proposal uniquely combines Anpo's expertise in tailoring surface sites with reactor engineering to address a fundamental scalability challenge.

proposals.title: Disentangling Photonic and Thermal Contributions: A Mechanistic Study of NO Oxidation over Surface-Engineered Photocatalysts
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic NOx removal is a promising green technology for air purification, but reported high activities under light often involve local heating, complicating mechanistic understanding. Pre-2026, the precise role of thermal energy versus photogenerated charge carriers in driving NO oxidation over advanced materials remains ambiguous, hindering rational catalyst design for pure solar-driven processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for catalysts like N-doped carbon-dots or phenolic resin-based systems, the primary NO oxidation pathway under visible light is genuine photocatalysis via surface charge transfer, and any observed thermal effects are synergistic rather than dominant.
proposals.method: We will synthesize model catalysts (e.g., Si/C-dots with N-C sites, phenolic resins with acid-base sites) based on Anpo's recent work. Using in-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) and online mass spectrometry, we will perform isotopic (18O2) tracing and quantitative activity measurements under controlled conditions: light-only, dark at elevated temperatures matching photothermal heating, and combined photo-thermal. Transient absorption spectroscopy will be employed to track photogenerated electron-hole pair dynamics and their correlation with reaction rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide definitive, mechanistic evidence distinguishing photocatalytic from thermal pathways in NO oxidation, establishing clear structure-activity relationships for surface sites. It will yield design principles for catalysts that maximize quantum efficiency for solar-driven air remediation.
proposals.novelty: While photo-thermal synergy is often invoked, this study systematically isolates and quantifies the photocatalytic contribution using surface-tailored catalysts and advanced in-situ spectroscopies, moving beyond correlative observations to causal mechanistic proof.

proposals.title: Au Nanoparticle-Decorated TiO₂ and WO₃ for Enhanced Photocatalytic NO Oxidation: Probing the Role of Semiconductor Support and Surface Acid-Base Sites
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic oxidation of nitric oxide (NO) is a crucial strategy for air purification, but efficiency remains limited by charge recombination and poor surface reaction kinetics. Pre-2026 work, including Anpo's recent studies, highlights the critical role of synergistic surface acid-base sites and metal co-catalysts in promoting photocatalytic aerobic oxidations. Extending this understanding to NO abatement by systematically varying the semiconductor support for Au nanoparticles addresses a significant environmental challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: The deposition of Au nanoparticles onto different semiconductor oxides (TiO₂, WO₃) will create distinct interfacial and surface acid-base properties, which will govern the efficiency of charge separation and surface oxygen activation, thereby controlling the photocatalytic NO oxidation activity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize Au nanoparticles (2-5 nm) on commercial and mesoporous TiO₂ (P25, anatase) and WO₃ using a controlled deposition-precipitation method, mirroring strategies from recent work on phenolic resin systems. Materials will be characterized by XRD, TEM, UV-Vis DRS, XPS, and in-situ FTIR to probe Au-semiconductor interfaces and surface sites. Photocatalytic NO oxidation will be tested under visible and UV light in a continuous flow reactor. In-situ spectroscopic techniques and controlled poisoning experiments will be used to correlate activity with specific Lewis/Brønsted acid-base sites and oxygen reduction pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a structure-activity relationship linking semiconductor support properties (band structure, surface acidity) with Au co-catalyst function in NO photo-oxidation. It will provide new design principles for hybrid photocatalysts in environmental remediation, specifically extending the concept of synergistic surface sites from organic oxidations to inorganic gas-phase reactions.
proposals.novelty: While Au/TiO₂ is known, a direct comparative study with WO₃ for NO oxidation, explicitly framed through the lens of tunable surface acid-base chemistry derived from the semiconductor support, is novel. This approach connects Anpo's recent focus on engineered surface sites with the well-established but underexplored variable of the bulk semiconductor in Au-hybrid systems.

proposals.title: Au Nanoparticles on Carbon Nitride with Engineered Lewis/Brønsted Acid-Base Sites for Dual-Function Photocatalysis: Hydrogen Evolution and Nitrogen Reduction
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution (HER) and nitrogen reduction (NRR) are critical for sustainable energy and ammonia production, but they suffer from low efficiency due to poor charge separation and sluggish surface kinetics. Pre-2026 work by Anpo's group has demonstrated that creating synergistic Lewis and Brønsted acid-base sites on carbon-based supports significantly enhances photocatalytic aerobic oxidations by facilitating proton-coupled electron transfer. Extending this proven surface engineering strategy to these more challenging reduction reactions could unlock new catalytic efficiencies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Au nanoparticles supported on carbon nitride (C3N4) with co-engineered Lewis/Brønsted acid-base sites will create a multifunctional photocatalytic system where the metal sites activate reactants and the tailored surface sites synergistically manage proton and electron transfers, thereby boosting both HER and NRR activities under visible light.
proposals.method: We will synthesize phenolic resin-derived carbon supports with in-situ generated acid-base sites, as per Anpo's recent methods, and use them to anchor graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4). Au nanoparticles will be deposited via photodeposition. The catalyst will be characterized using in-situ FTIR, XPS, and CO2/NH3-TPD to confirm acid-base properties and Au-C3N4 interaction. Photocatalytic HER will be tested in aqueous methanol, while NRR will be performed in N2-saturated water under visible light. Isotope-labeled 15N2 experiments and control tests will confirm NH3 production. Charge dynamics will be probed via time-resolved photoluminescence and transient absorption spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct link between engineered surface acid-base chemistry on carbon supports and the efficiency of photocatalytic reduction reactions, moving beyond their established role in oxidations. It will provide a versatile catalyst design principle for managing multi-step proton-electron transfers in small molecule activation, contributing to the rational development of dual-function photocatalysts.
proposals.novelty: While Au/C3N4 is known for HER, integrating it with the specific Lewis/Brønsted acid-base sites proven for oxidation catalysis to drive both HER and the thermodynamically demanding NRR on the same material is a novel and non-obvious application of Anpo's recent surface strategy.

proposals.title: Elucidating Deactivation Pathways and Enhancing the Long-Term Stability of Synergistic Lewis/Brønsted Acid-Base Photocatalysts for Aerobic NO Oxidation
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic NO oxidation is a promising strategy for air purification, but practical application is hindered by catalyst deactivation. While recent work by Anpo's group has developed highly active phenolic resin-based catalysts with synergistic Lewis/Brønsted acid-base sites, their long-term stability under continuous photocatalytic operation remains unexplored. Understanding the deactivation mechanisms is critical for designing durable, real-world catalysts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the primary deactivation mechanisms under prolonged NO oxidation involve the competitive adsorption and accumulation of nitrate by-products on the critical Lewis acid-base sites, coupled with photo-corrosion or carbonaceous deposition, which disrupts the synergistic surface chemistry essential for activating O₂ and NO.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of in-situ and post-reaction characterization. Long-term photocatalytic NO oxidation tests will be conducted in a continuous-flow reactor, monitoring activity over >50 hours. The catalyst will be periodically sampled and analyzed using in-situ CDIN (Chemical Deposition of Inorganic Nanostructures) to probe surface site evolution. Post-mortem analysis will involve XPS, FTIR, and TEM-EDS to identify chemical changes, adsorbed species, and structural degradation. Controlled poisoning experiments with nitrate ions and water vapor will isolate specific deactivation pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed mechanistic understanding of deactivation in this novel class of carbon-based photocatalysts for gas-phase oxidation. It will establish structure-stability relationships linking surface site synergy to durability, guiding the synthesis of more robust materials. The findings will advance the field from demonstrating high initial activity to ensuring sustained performance, a key step toward practical environmental remediation technologies.
proposals.novelty: While deactivation is commonly studied in metal oxide photocatalysts, systematic stability analysis of engineered polymeric carbon nitride/phenolic resin systems with designed dual acid-base sites is novel. The application of in-situ CDIN to track the fate of these specific functional sites during a gas-phase photocatalytic reaction represents a unique methodological approach.

proposals.title: Synergistic Lewis Acid-Base Sites on Carbon-Based Catalysts for the Photocatalytic Reduction of NO to N₂
proposals.motivation: Photocatalytic NO oxidation to nitrates is a well-studied air purification strategy. However, the selective photocatalytic reduction of NO to benign N₂ represents a more desirable, closed-loop solution for nitrogen management, yet it remains a significant challenge due to competing pathways and poor selectivity. Leveraging pre-2026 knowledge, this addresses the need for efficient, solar-driven denitrification processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that carbon-based catalysts (e.g., phenolic resin-derived materials or carbon-dots) featuring in-situ generated synergistic Lewis acid-base sites and surface N-C moieties will promote the multi-electron/proton transfer steps required for the selective photocatalytic reduction of NO to N₂, by effectively activating NO and stabilizing key intermediates.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of carbon-dot/phenolic resin composites via hydrothermal/pyrolysis methods, tuning surface chemistry to generate Lewis acid-base pairs and N-C sites, as demonstrated in Anpo's recent aerobic oxidation work. These will be characterized by in-situ CDIN, XPS, and FTIR to correlate active site structure with performance. Photocatalytic NO reduction activity and selectivity (N₂ vs. NH₃) will be evaluated in a continuous-flow reactor under UV/visible light, using H₂O or a sacrificial donor as the reductant, with products analyzed by GC and chemiluminescence.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new structure-activity relationship for photocatalytic NO reduction, moving beyond traditional oxidation frameworks. It will demonstrate the transferability of synergistic acid-base site design principles to reductive nitrogen chemistry, potentially leading to more efficient and selective denitrification photocatalysts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the recently developed strategy of creating synergistic Lewis acid-base sites—proven for aerobic oxidations—to the inverse, reductive problem of NO conversion. This shifts the focus from photocatalytic NO oxidation to its selective reduction, a less explored but critically important pathway.

proposals.title: Synergistic Lewis-Brønsted Acid Sites on Carbon-Dot Modified TiO2 for Photocatalytic Simultaneous Oxidation of NO and CO in Simulated Exhaust
proposals.motivation: The simultaneous removal of NO and CO from complex exhaust streams, such as those from diesel engines or industrial processes, remains a significant environmental challenge. Conventional thermal catalysts often suffer from poisoning, high energy costs, and inefficiency in oxidizing both pollutants under fluctuating conditions. Photocatalysis offers a low-energy pathway, but existing materials lack the tailored active sites to efficiently drive the oxidation of both NO (requiring oxygen activation) and CO (requiring electron transfer and oxygen supply) in a single system.
proposals.hypothesis: A carbon-dot modified TiO2 catalyst, engineered with co-existing Lewis and Brønsted acid sites on its surface, will create synergistic dual-function active centers that simultaneously and efficiently promote the photocatalytic oxidation of both NO and CO by enhancing oxygen activation, electron transfer, and reactant adsorption.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of TiO2-based photocatalysts using a sol-gel method, incorporating nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) derived from phenolic resins to generate surface N-C sites. In-situ generation of Lewis acid (Ti³⁺/oxygen vacancy) and Brønsted acid (surface -OH) sites will be achieved via controlled thermal treatment and surface modification, as per Anpo's recent strategies. The catalysts will be characterized using in-situ CDIN (Chemical Detection of Intermediates on the Nanoscale), FTIR, XPS, and EPR to confirm the acid site synergy and electron transfer dynamics. Photocatalytic performance for the simultaneous oxidation of NO and CO in a simulated exhaust stream (containing O2, H2O, and CO2) will be evaluated under UV-visible light.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for multifunctional photocatalysts by explicitly linking synergistic acid-base site pairs to dual-pollutant oxidation mechanisms. It will provide fundamental insights into how surface-engineered sites manage competing reactant pathways, advancing the field of environmental photocatalysis beyond single-target reactions. The developed catalyst system offers a potential blueprint for low-temperature, energy-efficient exhaust purification technologies.
proposals.novelty: While single-pollutant photocatalytic oxidation is well-studied, the deliberate design of a single photocatalyst with spatially and functionally coupled Lewis-Brønsted acid sites to drive two distinct oxidation reactions (NO and CO) simultaneously in a complex gas mixture is a novel and non-obvious strategy, moving beyond the typical focus on isolated active sites or single-reaction optimization.

ideas.title: Operando XAS Elucidation of Dynamic Basic Site Synergy in Photocatalytic Reductive Transformations
ideas.description: This research direction combines the design of photocatalysts with engineered Lewis and Brønsted basic sites for reductive chemistry with operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) to probe the system under working conditions. The core objective is to use operando XAS to monitor not just the chemical state of metallic co-catalysts, but to correlate these changes with the protonation state and electronic environment of the adjacent basic sites as the reaction proceeds. This allows for the direct observation of how substrate binding and activation at the basic sites modulate electron transfer from the photoexcited semiconductor to the co-catalyst, and vice-versa. The synergy creates a powerful feedback loop where spectroscopic insights guide the rational optimization of the basic site architecture for specific photocatalytic reductions beyond water splitting, such as CO2 or nitroarene reduction.

ideas.title: Adapting Photoreceptor Charge Separation to Drive Catalysis with Weak Absorbers
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that strong semiconductor light absorption is necessary for photothermal catalysis by drawing an analogy from biological photosynthesis. In photosynthesis, chlorophyll's efficient charge separation creates long-lived chemical potential (NADPH, proton gradient) that powers downstream thermal enzymes, decoupling light harvesting from catalytic energy needs. Adapting this, the research would investigate designing semiconductor heterojunctions or sensitized systems optimized purely for charge separation under weak light, even using indirect or sensitized absorption. The generated surface electrons and holes would act as mobile chemical reagents. Their injection into a reaction interface with synergistic acid sites would provide the activation energy thermally, allowing the acid synergy to drive the catalytic cycle using only the chemical potential of the light-generated charges, relaxing the need for the semiconductor itself to be a strong absorber or direct heater.

ideas.title: Bridging Real Sunlight and Controlled Laboratory Conditions for Reproducible Photocatalytic Benchmarking
ideas.description: This study evaluates the solar-to-hydrogen conversion efficiency of optimized PTI nanoplates by conducting a dual-path experiment that directly links outdoor testing with controlled laboratory validation. The photocatalytic system is first tested under real sunlight with simultaneous, high-resolution monitoring of the full solar spectrum, irradiance, and ambient conditions. The recorded spectral and intensity data are then used to precisely replicate the natural illumination profile using a tunable LED-based solar simulator for indoor testing of an identical photocatalyst sample. This approach yields a reproducible performance metric that isolates material properties from transient environmental factors, enabling robust benchmarking under realistic yet scientifically accountable conditions.

ideas.title: Defect-Engineered Co-Catalyst Interfaces for PTI Photocatalysis
ideas.description: This research direction integrates molecular passivation of PTI surface defects with the strategic optimization of CoOx and Pt/Cr2O3 co-catalyst loading and composition. The key synergy is that the passivation layer's chemical nature will directly inform the choice of co-catalyst precursors and deposition conditions to form an optimal, chemically bonded interface. This approach aims to simultaneously eliminate recombination sites on the PTI and create a co-catalyst interface with minimized electronic barriers, which neither strategy alone addresses. The expected outcome is a system where charge separation is enhanced both within the bulk (via passivation) and at the critical interface (via tailored co-catalyst integration), leading to superior and more stable photocatalytic performance.

ideas.title: Decoupled Light-Harvesting and Charge Injection to Drive Acid Synergy in Thermal Catalysis
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the fatal flaw of insufficient charge generation by separating the roles of light absorption and catalysis. A primary, strongly absorbing material harvests photons to generate a high flux of excited charges, which are then injected into a secondary, catalytically active component that is optimized for the thermal reaction and acid synergy. This architecture ensures a sufficient density of surface charges to electronically modify the catalytic active sites, enabling the light-driven promotion of the thermal cycle. The system is designed to rigorously quantify the injected charge flux and its direct correlation with enhancements in catalytic rate and selectivity.

ideas.title: Operando Raman spectroscopy for monitoring surface adsorbates and reaction intermediates during photocatalysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from heterogeneous thermal catalysis, where operando Raman spectroscopy is a established method for monitoring surface species. The analogous problem is understanding active sites and mechanisms under working conditions. The adapted research idea is to implement operando Raman spectroscopy during photocatalytic water splitting. By using a specially designed photocatalytic cell with optical access for illumination and Raman probe, one can monitor the chemical identity and dynamics of surface adsorbates and reaction intermediates on the photocatalyst in real-time under operational light and fluid conditions. This complements operando XAS data on co-catalyst oxidation states by directly probing the surface reaction pathway, helping to identify critical intermediates and deactivation mechanisms.

ideas.title: Molecularly Grafted Acidic Interfaces for Co-catalyst Integration and Activity Modulation
ideas.description: This research direction combines surface organometallic chemistry grafting with co-catalyst optimization by using the grafted Brønsted acid groups as molecular anchors and electronic modifiers for the subsequent deposition of CoOx and Pt/Cr2O3 co-catalysts. The controlled acid site density and strength will dictate the nucleation, dispersion, and interfacial electronic structure of the co-catalyst nanoparticles, moving beyond simple physical mixing to create chemically integrated hybrid sites. This approach allows for the systematic study of how proton transfer at the grafted acid sites couples with electron transfer at the metal-oxide co-catalysts, potentially unlocking new pathways for concerted proton-electron transfer in catalytic cycles. The result is a unified catalyst architecture where the support's surface chemistry is precisely engineered to optimize both the assembly and the cooperative function of multiple catalytic components.

ideas.title: In-situ spectroscopic mapping of molten salt copolymerization for targeted carbon nitride photocatalyst design
ideas.description: This research direction integrates molten salt copolymerization synthesis with advanced in-situ spectroscopy to move beyond trial-and-error exploration of binary precursors. By employing techniques like in-situ Raman or FTIR spectroscopy directly within the molten salt medium, the dynamic chemical interactions, intermediate formation, and the emergence of dual acid site motifs during the copolymerization process can be monitored in real time. This spectroscopic feedback will be used to rationally select and tune binary precursor pairs (e.g., adjusting ratios or introducing ternary components) to steer the polymerization pathway towards carbon nitride variants with optimized, synergistic active site architectures. The ultimate goal is to establish a causative link between specific precursor interactions during synthesis and the resulting photocatalyst's mechanistic performance, enabling the targeted design of materials for reactions like CO2 reduction or H2O2 production.

ideas.title: Single-Particle Interfacial Engineering via Site-Selective Co-catalyst Deposition
ideas.description: This research direction combines controlled, alternative co-catalyst deposition methods (e.g., impregnation, electrodeposition) with single-particle photocatalytic analysis. By applying different deposition techniques to individual, well-characterized catalyst nanoparticles, we can directly correlate the specific interfacial architecture created by each method with its photocatalytic performance, measured via single-molecule fluorescence microscopy. This allows for the unambiguous identification of which deposition strategy yields the most effective charge-transfer interfaces and active sites on a particle-by-particle basis. Consequently, the approach moves beyond comparing average performance of bulk samples to fundamentally understanding how synthetic control at the nanoscale dictates function at the single-active-site level.

ideas.title: Adapting Atomic Layer Deposition from Microelectronics for Co-catalyst Loading
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that co-catalysts must be deposited via photo-deposition by drawing an analogy from semiconductor microelectronics. In that field, Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD) solves the problem of creating perfectly uniform, conformal, and atomically precise thin films—a challenge analogous to achieving optimal co-catalyst dispersion and interface control. The adapted research idea is to employ ALD for co-catalyst loading. By using sequential, self-limiting gas-phase precursor reactions, this method could deposit co-catalysts (e.g., Pt, CoO_x, NiO_x) with unparalleled control over thickness, particle size, and distribution on the photocatalyst surface. This precision engineering may create more effective and tunable interfacial junctions for charge separation and transfer, potentially surpassing the performance of interfaces created by conventional photo-deposition, impregnation, or electrodeposition.

ideas.title: Defect-Passivated Packed-Bed Reactors for Continuous, Stable Photocatalytic Oxidation
ideas.description: This research direction designs a continuous-flow packed-bed reactor for photocatalytic oxidation where the PTI-based dual-acid catalyst is pre-treated and continuously exposed to a co-flow of molecular passivation agents, inspired by perovskite defect management. The passivators selectively pacify defect sites on the catalyst surface and edges within the packed bed, not just initially but throughout operation, mitigating deactivation pathways like active site blockage or structural degradation under constant flow and irradiation. This approach marries the scalability of flow chemistry with the stability-enhancing strategy of molecular passivation, targeting a reactor system that maintains high conversion efficiency and selectivity over extended durations, a critical step toward practical industrial application.

ideas.title: Single-Particle-Validated Design of Hierarchical Flow Reactors for Dual-Acid Photocatalysis
ideas.description: This research direction integrates single-particle photocatalytic studies with continuous flow reactor engineering to establish a closed-loop, multiscale design strategy. Single-molecule fluorescence microscopy is used to map the activity distribution and mechanistic pathways (e.g., dual-acid synergy) across a population of individual catalyst nanoparticles under simulated flow conditions. This fundamental heterogeneity data then directly informs the computational design and 3D printing of a hierarchically structured packed-bed reactor, where catalyst placement, pore architecture, and light distribution are optimized to match the observed kinetic profiles and mitigate performance bottlenecks. The resulting prototype flow reactor is validated for photocatalytic oxidation, creating a feedback loop where its performance data further refines the single-particle studies, ultimately yielding a scalable, rationally designed catalytic system with predictable industrial performance.

ideas.title: Solvent-Free Synthesis of Crystalline PTI via Inverted Acid-Site Catalysis
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a solvent-free synthesis of crystalline polymeric carbon nitride (PTI) by applying the inverted acid-site catalysis concept. In this system, solid Brønsted acid sites would be designed to activate a gaseous oxygen source, while adjacent Lewis acid sites coordinate and deprotonate the nitrogen-rich precursor (e.g., melamine or melem). This cooperative, inverted activation is hypothesized to drive the polycondensation and long-range order necessary for PTI crystallization entirely in the solid state, eliminating the need for both traditional solvents and eutectic molten salt fluxes. The core synergy is that the novel catalytic mechanism itself provides the directed chemical driving force and molecular mobility required for crystallization, which neither the search for an alternative medium nor the inverted acid-site concept alone could achieve.

ideas.title: Fluidized-bed photocatalytic reactor with integrated electrochemical catalyst regeneration for robust continuous oxidation
ideas.description: This redesigned system employs a fluidized-bed flow reactor to mitigate fouling and maintain uniform photon and reactant distribution. An integrated electrochemical cell continuously processes a slipstream of catalyst particles, applying a potential to oxidatively remove organic foulants and reductively reactivate metal centers in the dual-acid catalyst. This approach enables sustained, stable operation by decoupling the reaction kinetics from irreversible deactivation pathways. The system is designed for automated, closed-loop catalyst management, making it viable for long-term industrial application.

ideas.title: Predictive Design of Defect-Engineered Catalytic Sites in Polymeric Carbon Nitrides
ideas.description: This research moves beyond the simplistic question of whether defects can be beneficial to develop a predictive framework for defect engineering in polymeric carbon nitrides like PTI. It combines high-throughput density functional theory calculations to screen defect formation energies and catalytic activity descriptors with advanced operando characterization techniques to validate defect dynamics under reaction conditions. The goal is to create a defect-property map that rationally links specific, synthetically controllable defect types (e.g., nitrogen vacancies, metal dopants at specific sites) to enhanced and stable catalytic performance for targeted reactions such as hydrogen evolution or CO2 reduction. This approach transforms defect engineering from a trial-and-error process into a guided design principle for next-generation catalysts.

ideas.title: Computationally Guided Mechanochemical Synthesis of PTI-Supported Single-Atom Catalysts
ideas.description: This research direction integrates high-throughput DFT screening with solvent-free mechanochemical synthesis to create a novel platform for fabricating single-atom catalysts. First, computational screening is used to predict the most effective combinations of metal precursors (e.g., for Co, Ni, Fe) and solid Brønsted/Lewis acid co-catalysts that will bind strongly to the heptazine motifs of PTI and optimize catalytic sites. These predicted optimal 'precursor-acid pairs' are then subjected to one-pot ball milling with PTI precursors (like melem and LiCl/KCl salt), where the mechanical force simultaneously drives the polycondensation of crystalline PTI and the covalent grafting of the metal atoms onto its framework. This synergistic approach bypasses traditional solution-based impregnation and pyrolysis, allowing for the direct, room-temperature synthesis of atomically dispersed metal sites with tailored local coordination and adjacent acid sites, ideal for probing structure-activity relationships in challenging reactions like CO2 reduction or methane activation.

ideas.title: Apply High-Throughput Formulation Screening and Synergistic Pairing Analysis to Co-Catalyst Optimization
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from pharmaceutical combination therapy optimization, this idea adapts high-throughput formulation screening and synergistic pairing analysis to the problem of co-catalyst optimization. In drug development, optimal multi-drug ratios are found by screening compound libraries and analyzing dose-response matrices for synergy (e.g., using the Chou-Talalay method). Adapted here, a combinatorial library of CoOx and Pt/Cr2O3 co-catalysts with varied loadings and compositions will be synthesized on a standard photocatalyst substrate. A high-throughput photocatalytic testing platform will rapidly assay hydrogen evolution rates. The resulting activity data will be analyzed using a framework adapted for photocatalysis (e.g., a photocatalytic combination index) to rigorously identify specific loadings and ratios where the co-catalysts exhibit synergistic, non-additive enhancement of charge separation and overall activity, moving beyond simple one-variable-at-a-time optimization.

ideas.title: Adapting NMR-based protein-ligand mapping to quantify photocatalyst adsorption sites
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from structural biology—where NMR spectroscopy with isotopically labeled components is used to map and quantify protein-ligand binding interfaces—to the problem of characterizing dual acid sites on photocatalysts. The analogous solution involves using specific isotopic labeling (e.g., 13C, 15N, or D) of probe molecules (like pyridine or ammonia) combined with solid-state NMR and IR spectroscopy. The adapted research idea applies this to photocatalysts by introducing these labeled probes, enabling the deconvolution of overlapping spectroscopic signals. This allows researchers to precisely map, assign, and quantify the strength and population of distinct Brønsted and Lewis acid sites on photocatalyst surfaces, providing a more detailed picture of the active sites crucial for photocatalytic reactions.

ideas.title: Grafted Adaptive Acid Sites with Stimuli-Responsive Ligand Spheres
ideas.description: This approach uses surface organometallic chemistry to graft well-defined metal complexes that serve as precursors for Brønsted acid sites. Crucially, these complexes are functionalized with covalently attached, stimuli-responsive molecular ligands (e.g., photochromic or redox-active groups) in the secondary coordination sphere. Under operational conditions, applying a specific external trigger reversibly alters the ligand's conformation or electronic state, which in turn modulates the strength and accessibility of the proton at the grafted site. This results in a heterogeneous catalyst whose acid properties can be dynamically tuned on-demand to enhance reaction rates, switch selectivity, or improve stability against deactivation.

ideas.title: Adapting Dual-Acid Synergy for Alternative Oxidants via Enzyme Cofactor Analogy
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept by using an analogy from bioinorganic chemistry. In enzyme catalysis, a metal center's environment is precisely engineered to activate specific peroxide oxidants. Adapting this solution, the research would explore if a synthetic dual-acid synergy (e.g., a Lewis acid metal site paired with a Brønsted acid site) on a solid catalyst can be tuned to enhance the activation of alternative oxidants like hydrogen peroxide or organic peroxides, moving beyond molecular oxygen. The work would involve designing catalyst libraries where the identity, strength, and proximity of the two acid sites are varied to find optimal configurations for cleaving the O-O bond in each target oxidant, aiming for higher efficiency and selectivity in oxidation reactions.

ideas.title: Computational Discovery of Single-Precursor Activation Pathways for High-Crystallinity Materials
ideas.description: This research direction merges the goal of single-precursor crystallinity with computational screening of acid catalysts. Instead of screening acid pairs to co-process two separate precursors, high-throughput DFT is used to screen how hypothetical Lewis/Brønsted acid combinations on a target semiconductor surface can catalytically activate and rearrange bonds *within a single, complex precursor molecule*. The computational workflow predicts which catalytic surface environments can guide the precursor through a stepwise reaction pathway that directly yields a crystalline framework, such as PTI, from a monolithic starting material. This enables the discovery of novel, atom-efficient synthesis routes that are not intuitive from classical precursor chemistry.

ideas.title: Computationally Guided, Defect-Motif-Specific Passivation for Carbon Nitride (PTI)
ideas.description: This redesigned approach abandons the direct analogy to perovskites and instead establishes a first-principles methodology for passivating polymeric carbon nitrides like PTI. It begins with ab initio modeling to map the distinct defect landscape of PTI, identifying the precise chemical nature of charge-trapping sites. A virtual screening pipeline then evaluates candidate passivation agents from a library designed for robust covalent/coordination chemistry with nitrogen-rich carbon networks, not ionic crystals. The highest-ranking candidates are experimentally tested on synthetically controlled PTI with known defect densities, using correlated spectroscopy and performance metrics to validate defect-specific pacification and its impact on photophysical properties.

ideas.title: Computational Screening of Dynamic Acid-Passivator Complexes for Defect-Free PTI Catalysis
ideas.description: This research direction employs high-throughput DFT screening to identify optimal dual-acid catalytic sites on PTI surfaces, but uniquely expands the search to include molecular passivation agents (e.g., Lewis bases like thiophenes or ammonium salts) as integral components of the active site. The computational workflow will model how specific Brønsted/Lewis acid pairs interact with candidate passivators to form a stabilized 'active-passivated complex,' where the passivator selectively pacifies neighboring detrimental defects without poisoning the catalytic acid centers. This allows for the in-silico design of a fully coordinated surface where high activity and low charge recombination are achieved concurrently from the same molecular architecture. The predicted optimal complexes will then be synthesized and tested, aiming to translate a holistic surface design principle from perovskite photovoltaics to photocatalytic polymer networks.

ideas.title: Interface-First Co-catalyst Integration via Property-Matched Deposition
ideas.description: This research abandons the assumption that any single deposition method is universally optimal. Instead, it establishes a framework where the ideal electronic and chemical interface for a given host/co-catalyst pair is defined first through simulation and surface science. A suite of deposition techniques is then deployed as tools to achieve that target interface, with their parameters tuned based on the system's specific needs. The outcome is a rational selection guide linking material properties to the most effective loading method, moving beyond trial-and-error to predictive catalyst design.

ideas.title: Applying Battery Electrode Degradation Protocols to Assess PTI Photocatalyst Stability
ideas.description: This idea adapts the rigorous, mechanism-focused degradation testing protocols from lithium-ion battery electrode research to the study of crystalline PTI photocatalysts. In batteries, standardized accelerated aging tests (high-rate cycling, elevated temperature) are combined with detailed post-mortem material characterization (SEM, XRD, XPS) to distinguish between different failure modes like mechanical fracture, surface passivation, or bulk phase change. This approach is adapted by designing an accelerated stress test for PTI that subjects it to harsh but relevant conditions—rapid thermal swings, alternating UV-visible irradiation, and cycling in reactive media—to compress long-term operational time. After defined stress intervals, the material undergoes a 'post-mortem' analysis using identical characterization tools to correlate specific structural or chemical changes (e.g., crystallinity loss, surface contamination, pore collapse) with the measured decline in photocatalytic activity. This method moves beyond simple activity measurements over cycles to diagnose the root cause of degradation, directly informing the design of more robust PTI architectures or regeneration protocols.

ideas.title: Optimizing π-Conjugation Length in PTI by Balancing Absorption Spectral Range Against Exciton Dissociation Efficiency
ideas.description: This study moves beyond the simplistic 'longer is better' paradigm by investigating the fundamental trade-off between extended π-conjugation for improved light absorption and the detrimental increase in exciton binding energy that hinders charge generation. A series of PTI-based materials with systematically varied conjugation lengths will be synthesized and characterized. Their performance will be evaluated not just by mobility and absorption, but crucially by direct spectroscopic measurement of exciton binding energy and free charge yield. The goal is to identify the conjugation length that provides the optimal compromise between broad absorption and efficient exciton dissociation for high-performance organic photovoltaics or photodetectors.

ideas.title: In-situ PCVD and rapid thermal annealing for crystalline PTI film synthesis and integration
ideas.description: This approach uses a pulsed-chemical vapor deposition (PCVD) system coupled with an integrated rapid thermal annealing (RTA) module to synthesize crystalline polymeric carbon nitride (PTI) films. Volatile precursors like cyanamide are pulsed onto a heated substrate in a controlled ammonia atmosphere to form an intermediate pre-polymer film. Immediately following deposition, the film undergoes a brief RTA step in the same reactor environment to drive the condensation polymerization into the crystalline PTI phase, optimizing its electronic structure for charge transport. This closed-loop, two-step process ensures reproducible growth of adherent, photoactive PTI films directly on conductive substrates for integrated photoelectrodes.

ideas.title: Single-Precursor PTI Synthesis with In-Situ Passivation via a Reactive Molecular Agent
ideas.description: This research direction proposes synthesizing crystalline polymeric carbon nitride (PTI) from a single molecular precursor, such as melamine, but introducing a reactive passivation agent during the thermal condensation process. The agent, inspired by perovskite passivators like Lewis bases or halide salts, is designed to not only bind to and pacify defect sites (e.g., terminal amines, uncondensed bonds) but also to participate in or catalyze the condensation reaction itself, lowering the energy barrier for crystalline order. This results in a one-pot synthesis where the passivator acts as a 'molecular director,' simultaneously promoting long-range order and healing intrinsic defects as the material forms, rather than as a post-synthetic treatment. The outcome is a simplified, potentially lower-temperature route to high-quality, defect-minimized PTI from a single source, merging synthetic simplicity with superior electronic structure control.

Adapted to photocatalysis, the research would employ a nanoscale pH sensor (e.g., a nanoelectrode or a confined fluorescence probe) positioned at the surface of an individual catalyst nanoparticle. This method would allow for the direct, real-time observation and quantification of proton and hydronium ion production rates from distinct acidic sites, moving beyond ensemble averages to reveal the precise mechanistic interplay and spatiotemporal dynamics of the dual-acid synergy at the single-particle level.
